title: How to manage AI investments in the agentic era
authors: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI outlines a framework for enterprises to evaluate and manage AI investments in systems built around autonomous agents. The post emphasizes measuring return on investment through "useful work per dollar" rather than traditional compute metrics, and proposes strategies for improving efficiency and scaling workflows that deliver business value.

title: ChatGPT is now a partner for your most ambitious work
authors: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI has released ChatGPT Work, an agentic version of ChatGPT capable of taking autonomous actions across applications and files to complete complex, multi-step projects over extended periods. This represents a shift from conversational AI to task-oriented agents that can persist on goals and execute workflows independently.

title: Native-speed vLLM transformers modeling backend
authors: Hugging Face
summary: Hugging Face has released a native-speed vLLM transformers modeling backend, integrating vLLM's high-performance inference engine directly with the transformers library. This eliminates performance bottlenecks in serving large language models by enabling transformers models to run at vLLM's optimized speeds without separate conversion or bridging layers.

title: Data for Agents
authors: Hugging Face
summary: This post from Hugging Face addresses the critical role of data quality and curation in building effective autonomous agents. The core message is that agent performance is fundamentally limited by the datasets used for training and fine-tuning, making data strategy as important as model architecture itself.

title: ScarfBench: Benchmarking AI Agents for Enterprise Java Framework Migration
authors: Hugging Face
summary: ScarfBench is a benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents' ability to perform enterprise Java framework migration tasks. This addresses a critical gap in agent evaluation by testing real-world, complex software engineering challenges that require multi-step reasoning, code understanding, and refactoring capabilities.

title: DiScoFormer: One transformer for density and score, across distributions
authors: Hugging Face
summary: DiScoFormer is a unified transformer architecture that handles both density estimation and score-based modeling across different distributions. This work addresses the challenge of building flexible generative models that can seamlessly work with multiple probabilistic frameworks without requiring separate specialized architectures.

title: Using Local Coding Agents
authors: Ahead of AI (Sebastian Raschka)
summary: This post explores using open-weight language models deployed locally as coding agents, positioning them as a cost-effective alternative to subscription-based services like Claude Code and Codex. The focus is on practical implementation of self-hosted models within local development environments rather than relying on proprietary cloud APIs.

title: How agents are transforming work
authors: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI's research demonstrates that AI agents are becoming practical tools for executing extended, multi-step workflows and complex tasks across diverse professional domains. This shift from single-turn interactions to autonomous task execution represents a meaningful expansion of AI's role in workplace productivity.

title: Accelerating Transformers Fine-Tuning with NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel
authors: Hugging Face
summary: This post describes NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel, a tool designed to accelerate the fine-tuning process for transformer-based language models. The announcement highlights performance improvements and simplified workflows for practitioners looking to adapt pre-trained models to downstream tasks more efficiently.

title: Experimenting with the proposed Cross-Origin Storage API in Transformers.js
authors: Hugging Face
summary: Hugging Face is experimenting with a proposed Cross-Origin Storage API feature in Transformers.js, their JavaScript library for running transformer models in the browser. This work focuses on improving how browser-based transformer models can persist and access data across origins, enabling more efficient model deployment and inference in web environments.

title: GLM-5.2 is the step change for open agents
authors: Interconnects (Nathan Lambert)
summary: GLM-5.2 represents a significant capability breakthrough for autonomous agents, crossing a critical performance threshold that enables more reliable and capable independent operation. The post highlights that this model marks a qualitative shift in what open-source agent systems can accomplish.

title: MosaicLeaks: Can your research agent keep a secret?
authors: Hugging Face
summary: MosaicLeaks examines security vulnerabilities in research agents—specifically whether autonomous agents can be trusted to handle sensitive information without leaking it. The post highlights risks in current agent architectures that may inadvertently expose confidential data through logs, memory systems, or inter-agent communication.

title: Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling
authors: Hugging Face
summary: This post addresses the challenge of evaluating whether open-source language models have sufficient agentic capabilities for real-world applications. Rather than relying on generic benchmarks, it proposes assessing models against your own tooling and workflows to determine if they can handle autonomous task execution in your specific context.

title: From the Hugging Face Hub to robot hardware with Strands Agents and LeRobot
authors: Hugging Face
summary: Hugging Face has integrated Strands Agents and LeRobot to enable seamless deployment of AI models from the Hugging Face Hub directly onto robot hardware. This bridges the gap between model development and physical robotics applications, making it easier for researchers and practitioners to move trained models into real-world robotic systems.

title: Agentic Resource Discovery: Let agents search
authors: Hugging Face

title: Securing the future of AI agents
authors: Google DeepMind
summary: Google DeepMind outlines a systematic approach to securing AI agent systems through an "AI Control Roadmap" that integrates traditional safety mechanisms with continuous real-time monitoring. The work addresses the critical challenge of ensuring AI agents remain controllable and safe as they operate autonomously in complex environments.

title: New OpenAI Academy courses for the next era of work
authors: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI has launched three new Academy courses designed to teach practical skills for working with AI systems, including building workflows and deploying agents. These courses address the growing need for hands-on training as AI tools become embedded in everyday professional work.

title: OpenAI to acquire Ona
authors: OpenAI
summary: OpenAI is acquiring Ona to integrate secure, persistent cloud environments into Codex, its code generation model. This acquisition enables long-running AI agents to operate reliably within enterprise workflows, extending beyond single-session code execution.

title: Investing in multi-agent AI safety research
authors: Google DeepMind
summary: Google DeepMind is launching a $10M funding initiative dedicated to multi-agent AI safety research. This represents a significant institutional commitment to understanding and mitigating risks that emerge when multiple AI agents interact in shared environments.

title: How an Agent Built a 3D Paris Gallery by Chaining Two Hugging Face Spaces
authors: Hugging Face
summary: A Hugging Face agent successfully chained together two separate Spaces (hosted ML applications) to autonomously generate and render a 3D virtual gallery of Paris. This demonstrates practical agentic behavior—breaking down a complex creative task into subtasks and orchestrating multiple specialized models in sequence.

title: Introducing Gemma 4 12B: a unified, encoder-free multimodal model
authors: Google DeepMind
summary: Google DeepMind has released Gemma 4 12B, a unified multimodal model that processes both text and images without a separate encoder. This represents a shift toward simpler, more efficient architectures that handle multiple modalities in a single unified framework.

title: The Open Source Community is backing OpenEnv for Agentic RL
authors: Hugging Face
summary: The open-source community is rallying around OpenEnv, a framework designed for training and evaluating agentic reinforcement learning systems. This signals growing momentum behind standardized, community-driven tooling for agent-based RL, addressing a gap in accessible infrastructure for this emerging capability area.

title: Do AI Agents Know When a Task Is Simple? Toward Complexity-Aware Reasoning and Execution
authors: Junjie Yin, Xinyu Feng
summary: This paper identifies a critical inefficiency in LLM-based agents: they often over-read context and re-examine already-seen information, turning simple tasks into expensive operations. The authors propose E3, a three-step framework (Estimate, Execute, Expand) that teaches agents to first judge task complexity, execute minimally, and only expand scope when needed—achieving 85% cost reduction and 91% fewer tokens while maintaining 100% success on code-editing benchmarks.

title: TerraZero: Procedural Driving Simulation for Zero-Demonstration Self-Play at Scale
authors: Zhouchonghao Wu, Akshay Rangesh, Weixin Li, Wei-Jer Chang, Zachary Lee et al.
summary: TerraZero is a fast procedural driving simulator that trains autonomous driving agents using reinforcement learning and self-play, without any human demonstrations. The system runs 1.3M simulation steps per second on a single GPU while maintaining realistic traffic rules, multiple agent types, and diverse scenarios — and achieves state-of-the-art performance on autonomous driving benchmarks, including zero-shot generalization to unseen cities.

title: PalmClaw: A Native On-Device Agent Framework for Mobile Phones
authors: Hongru Cai, Yongqi Li, Ran Wei, Wenjie Li
summary: PalmClaw is a framework that runs AI agents directly on mobile phones, letting them control device features and complete multi-step tasks without sending data to servers. Unlike existing mobile agents that click buttons and swipe screens, PalmClaw exposes device capabilities (like contacts, calendar, sensors) as explicit tools with clear inputs and outputs, achieving 11.5% better task success and 95% faster execution.

title: Privacy Attacks on Stable Marriage
authors: Stephan A. Fahrenkrog-Petersen, Aleksander Figiel, Darya Melnyk, Tijana Milentijević, Stefan Schmid
summary: This paper reveals that the Gale-Shapley stable marriage algorithm — widely used in real-world matching systems like medical residency placement — leaks private preference information when one side can repeatedly query the algorithm. The authors show attackers can fully reconstruct honest participants' preferences through strategic interactions, highlighting a critical privacy vulnerability in a foundational algorithm used in sensitive domains.

title: The Spectrum Is Not Enough: When Context Helps Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Mert Onur Cakiroglu, Mehmet Dalkilic, Hasan Kurban
summary: This paper challenges the common assumption that spectral indices (mathematical measures of a time series' predictability based on frequency content) can tell us whether adding context—like longer lookback windows or retrieval systems—will improve forecasting. The authors prove this is mathematically impossible and introduce a new diagnostic called "coverage deficit" to determine when context actually helps. Their work matters because practitioners often misuse spectral indices to make deployment decisions, potentially wasting resources on methods that won't work for their data.

title: Watermark Forensics for Generative Models: An Information-Theoretic Perspective
authors: Xiaoyu Li, Zheng Gao, Xiaoyan Feng, Jiaojiao Jiang, Yulei Sui et al.
summary: This paper analyzes watermarks in generative model outputs through an information-theoretic lens, asking what it costs (in tokens) to detect whether text is machine-made, attribute it to a specific user, extract hidden payloads, or localize edited portions. The key finding is a tight asymptotic law: attributing text to one of N users requires Θ(log N / h) tokens on average, where h is the source entropy rate — a fundamental limit that applies universally to stationary sources and holds for major language models like GPT-2 and Qwen2.5.

title: FormalAnalyticGeo: A Neural-Symbolic Based Framework for Multimodal Analytic Geometry Problem Generation
authors: Ruoran Xu, Wending Gao, Qiufeng Wang
summary: This paper presents FormalAnalyticGeo, a framework that automatically generates thousands of multimodal analytic geometry problems (problems combining text, diagrams, and equations). The system uses large language models coordinated through a formal intermediate language to generate problems and render precise diagrams, then verifies correctness automatically—solving the bottleneck of human annotation that has limited progress in training math-reasoning AI systems on geometry.

title: Ensemble Controlled-Flow Filtering for Implicit Data Assimilation
authors: Zhuoyuan Li, Yue Zhao, Ming Li
summary: This paper introduces a new method for data assimilation — the problem of combining noisy observations with dynamical system models to estimate true system state. The key innovation is handling "implicit" observations (those defined only through simulation, non-smooth, or many-to-one mappings) that existing methods cannot easily process. The authors propose Ensemble Controlled-flow Filter (EnCF), which frames state estimation as tilting probability distributions guided by observation energy, and prove it produces exact updates under ideal conditions.

title: The Illusion of Robustness: Aggregate Accuracy Hides Prediction Flips under Task-Irrelevant Context
authors: Yanzhe Zhang, Sanmi Koyejo, Diyi Yang
summary: This paper reveals a hidden vulnerability in large language models: while they appear robust to irrelevant context at the aggregate level (overall accuracy stays stable), individual examples show dramatic prediction flips. Even nonsensical text can flip model predictions on specific examples, creating "tail risks" masked by standard benchmarking. This matters because deployed LLMs operate in context-rich settings where such hidden instabilities could cause real failures.

title: Knowledge- and Gradient-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Parametrized Action Markov Decision Processes
authors: Jonas Ehrhardt, René Heesch, Oliver Niggemann
summary: This paper tackles reinforcement learning in environments where actions have two parts: a symbolic choice (which action to take) and numerical parameters (how to configure it). The key innovation is combining domain knowledge — rules, constraints, expert heuristics — with gradient-based optimization to make agents learn faster and make better decisions. By integrating symbolic knowledge with neural learning, the method achieves better sample efficiency and higher rewards than existing approaches.

title: Contrastive-Collapsed Loss for Flexible and Geometrically Optimal Embeddings and Faster Convergence
authors: Blanca Cano-Camarero, Ángela Fernández-Pascual, José R. Dorronsoro
summary: This paper introduces CoCo, a new loss function for training neural networks to learn better representations—compact clusters of similar examples with clear separation between different classes. The key innovation is balancing two competing goals: pulling same-class examples together while pushing different classes apart, while still allowing the network flexibility to find geometrically optimal solutions. The authors show theoretically and empirically that CoCo converges faster and produces tighter class clustering than standard approaches like cross-entropy.

title: A 32-channel event-based bio-signal analog front-end with adaptive delta and pulse frequency encoding
authors: Narayanan Shyam, Saptarshi Ghosh, Giacomo Indiveri
summary: This paper describes a specialized computer chip (ASIC) that reads biological signals from 32 sensors and converts them into sparse, event-based data streams using two encoding methods. The innovation is an adaptive encoder that automatically adjusts compression based on signal properties, enabling ultra-low-power wireless transmission of brain signals for brain-computer interfaces and neuromorphic computing systems.

title: MemOps: Benchmarking Lifecycle Memory Operations in Long-Horizon Conversations
authors: Xixuan Hao, Zeyu Zhang, Zehao Lin, Yihang Sun, Ziliang Guo et al.
summary: This paper introduces MemOps, a benchmark for evaluating how well LLM-based agents manage memory across long, multi-turn conversations. Instead of just checking if a final answer is correct, MemOps breaks down memory failures into specific operation types — remembering, forgetting, updating, reflecting — and traces exactly where and why systems fail to maintain consistent memory states during extended interactions.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Misconceptions in Multi-Turn Medical Conversations
authors: Monica Munnangi, Saiph Savage
summary: This paper evaluates whether large language models can reliably detect and correct medical misconceptions during multi-turn conversations with patients. The authors introduce ThReadMed-QA, a dataset of 2,437 real patient-physician conversation threads, and find that even frontier models (like GPT-4 and Claude) drop from ~85% accuracy in correcting misconceptions on the first question to ~50% by the second or third follow-up, revealing a critical safety gap in patient-facing medical AI.

title: MetaInfer: A Knowledge Only LLM Inference Engine Generator SKILL Toolbox
authors: Zhenwen Miao, Honglin Wang, Mingheng Mi
summary: MetaInfer is a system that uses LLMs and multi-agent collaboration to automatically generate customized inference engines for running large language models. Instead of building general-purpose frameworks that support every possible hardware, quantization scheme, and optimization strategy (which becomes increasingly complex), users specify only their runtime constraints, and the system generates a minimal, specialized inference engine that meets those needs. This matters because LLM inference is becoming a critical production bottleneck—the explosion of hardware variants and optimization techniques makes maintaining general frameworks unsustainably expensive.

title: Deep4ge: DNN Training Trajectories for Fault Detection and Diagnosis
authors: Sigma Jahan
summary: This paper introduces Deep4ge, a public dataset of 14,227 deep learning training runs with documented faults, designed to enable automated detection and diagnosis of bugs in neural network code. The dataset captures per-epoch training metrics and behavioral features, addressing a gap in software engineering tools for debugging deep learning systems by providing researchers with real faults injected into TensorFlow/Keras programs.

title: Can LLMs Write Reliable Rubrics? A Meta-Evaluation for Experiment Reproduction
authors: Hanhua Hong, Yizhi Li, Jiaoyan Chen, Luu Gia Huy, Sophia Ananiadou et al.
summary: This paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) can automatically generate reliable evaluation rubrics for assessing research paper reproduction tasks. The authors conduct the first systematic evaluation of LLM-generated rubrics, showing that while these rubrics can approach human-quality performance in scoring alignment, they have significant limitations including over-specificity and scoring bias. This matters because manual rubric creation is a bottleneck preventing scalable benchmarking of LLM research agents.

title: ANGLE: Angular Neural Generative Learning via Engression
authors: Rajdeep Pathak, Archi Roy, Tanujit Chakraborty
summary: This paper introduces ANGLE, a deep generative framework for predicting circular data (angles, directions) and their full probability distributions rather than just point estimates. Traditional regression fails on circular data because the geometric mean of angles is often meaningless; ANGLE learns the complete conditional distribution using a novel loss function and proves it has desirable mathematical properties like rotational invariance, with applications to pose estimation and wind prediction.

title: Accelerating Masked Diffusion Large Language Models: A Survey of Efficient Inference Techniques
authors: Daehoon Gwak, Minhyung Lee, Junwoo Park, Jaegul Choo
summary: This paper surveys efficient inference techniques for diffusion language models (dLLMs)—a newer class of language models that generate text in parallel rather than one token at a time. While parallel generation offers theoretical speed advantages over standard autoregressive models, the paper shows that realizing these gains requires careful optimization across algorithms, hardware, and system design. The authors provide a framework to measure and compare these techniques rigorously.

title: Who Grades the Grader? Co-Evolving Evaluation Metrics and Skills for Self-Improving LLM Agents
authors: Xing Zhang, Guanghui Wang, Yanwei Cui, Ziyuan Li, Wei Qiu et al.
summary: This paper solves a bootstrapping problem: how do self-improving AI agents evaluate their own progress when no reliable evaluation metric exists? The authors propose **Double Ratchet**, a system that co-evolves evaluation metrics alongside agent skills, using evolutionary search to build transparent metrics from simple components rather than relying on opaque judges. They show this approach recovers 88–110% of the performance gain that would come from having perfect ground-truth evaluation, across code generation, SQL query synthesis, and report generation tasks.

title: Internet of Agentic Things: Networked AI Agents for Closed-Loop IoT Orchestration
authors: Quanyan Zhu
summary: This paper proposes the Internet of Agentic Things (IoAT), a framework that deploys autonomous AI agents across distributed IoT devices, edge computers, and cloud systems to coordinate physical infrastructure in real time. Rather than treating IoT as passive sensors and actuators, IoAT gives agents the ability to perceive environments, reason about goals, and execute control decisions across cyber-physical systems — creating a closed-loop orchestration system. The work matters because it addresses how to scale intelligent coordination to complex, distributed physical systems like smart buildings while handling safety, security, and trustworthiness constraints.

title: Fisher Rank Inflation: A Spectral Signature of Memorization under Label Noise
authors: Satwik Bathula, Anand A. Joshi
summary: This paper identifies "Fisher Rank Inflation" — a spectral pattern in neural network gradients that reveals when models transition from learning clean structure to memorizing mislabeled data. By analyzing the effective rank of per-example gradients in the last layer, the authors show that noisy labels temporarily inflate this rank, creating a detectable signature that tracks memorization and correlates with corruption severity.

title: PolarBM: Complex-valued Boltzmann Machine for Modeling Audio Signals in Polar and Log-polar Coordinates
authors: Toru Nakashika, Kohei Yatabe
summary: This paper introduces PolarBM, a Boltzmann machine designed to naturally handle complex-valued data (numbers with both magnitude and phase) using polar coordinates instead of converting to real numbers. The key insight is that amplitude and phase are physically linked in signals like audio spectrograms, and modeling this relationship directly improves performance on tasks like audio modeling compared to standard neural networks that treat them independently.

title: XScientist: A Git-Like Research Protocol for Long-Running Autonomous Scientific Discovery
authors: Jixiang Luo
summary: XScientist is a version-control system (like Git) designed to manage autonomous AI systems that conduct scientific research over long periods. Instead of treating research as a one-shot output (a PDF paper), it records the entire exploration process—failed experiments, repairs, revisions, and claims—as an auditable artifact that humans and AI can inspect, verify, and build upon.

title: Learning the Graphical Nature of Symmetries
authors: Rashid Barket, Enrico Grimaldi, Yacoub Hendi, Edward Hirst, Adam Onus et al.
summary: This paper creates a large dataset of Cayley graphs (network representations of finite groups) and uses machine learning to predict algebraic group properties from graph structure alone. The work demonstrates that graph neural networks can learn hidden mathematical patterns in these symmetry structures, bridging abstract algebra and modern deep learning.

title: Requential Coding: Pushing the Limits of Model Compression with Self-Generated Training Data
authors: Shikai Qiu, Marc Finzi, Yujia Zheng, Kun Zhang, Andrew Gordon Wilson
summary: This paper introduces "requential coding," a new compression method that measures how well a student neural network can reproduce samples selected by a teacher model trained on the same data. Unlike prior compression techniques that struggle with model size or data entropy, requential coding produces code lengths that depend only on disagreement between teacher and student—enabling dramatically shorter codes and tighter generalization bounds for billion-parameter language models.

title: Invariant Learning Dynamics of Transformers in Inductive Reasoning Tasks
authors: Tiberiu Musat, Tiago Pimentel, Nicholas Zucchet, Thomas Hofmann
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework explaining how Transformer language models learn to perform inductive reasoning tasks by discovering that their training dynamics evolve on a low-dimensional "invariant manifold" — a constrained mathematical surface where complex learning can be captured by just a handful of interpretable coordinates instead of millions of parameters. This is important because it moves us toward predictive theories of *how* Transformers learn, bridging the gap between empirical observation and rigorous mathematical explanation.

title: A Minimalist Retargeting-Guided Reinforcement Learning Recipe for Dexterous Manipulation
authors: Yunhai Feng, Natalie Leung, Jiaxuan Wang, Lujie Yang, Haozhi Qi et al.
summary: This paper presents REGRIND, a method for teaching robots dexterous manipulation (like using scissors or screwdrivers) by learning from human demonstrations combined with reinforcement learning. The approach retargets human hand motions to robot references, trains a policy in simulation to track object-centric landmarks, and transfers it directly to real hardware—achieving fluid, human-like behavior without extensive real-world retraining.

title: A Durability and Cross-Language Transfer Benchmark for a Validated Teaching-Feedback Classification Protocol
authors: Esteban U. Vega Barajas
summary: This paper validates that an older protocol for classifying teaching feedback (sorting comments by theme and sentiment) remains effective even as AI models have advanced dramatically from 2019 to 2026. The key finding: you don't need the latest frontier models—cheaper models work just as well for this task, and the protocol transfers reasonably to English, making it a durable and practical tool for institutions analyzing feedback at scale.

title: Inside the Unfair Judge: A Mechanistic Interpretability Account of LLM-as-Judge Bias
authors: Zixiang Xu, Sixian Li, Huaxing Liu, Xiang Wang, Shuai Li et al.
summary: This paper explains *why* language models produce biased judgments by examining their internal neural representations rather than just their outputs. The authors show that bias manifests as a consistent geometric pattern in the model's hidden layers—a low-dimensional "bias direction"—and demonstrate they can both steer the model along this direction to induce or remove bias and predict bias failures on unseen tasks by detecting this geometry.

title: Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering
authors: Shijie Wang, Honglu Zhou, Ziyang Wang, Ran Xu, Caiming Xiong et al.
summary: This paper introduces Evidence-Backed Video Question Answering (E-VQA), a task that requires video AI models to answer questions while also providing visual proof — temporal segments and pixel-level masks showing what they "looked at" to answer. The authors find that current video language models can answer questions accurately without truly understanding the visual content, and propose a new dataset and training approach to fix this gap.

title: Can LLMs Perform Deep Technical Comprehension of Computer Architecture Papers?
authors: Nishant Aggarwal, Ayushi Dubal, Sreeraj Kannakarankodi, Ian McDougall, Adarsh Mittal et al.
summary: This paper evaluates whether large language models can perform rigorous technical analysis of computer architecture research papers—going beyond summarization to produce structured critique that identifies core mechanisms, hidden assumptions, and broader implications. The authors built Gauntlet, a multi-agent pipeline with five expert-persona reviewers and an adversarial synthesis stage, and found that on 20 top-tier papers, human reviewers preferred Gauntlet's analysis in 15 cases, with significant advantages in critical rigor.

title: AdvancedMathBench: A Benchmark Suite for Advanced Mathematical Proof Generation and Verification
authors: Lingkai Kong, Zijian Wu, Yuzhe Gu, Haiteng Zhao, Wenyong Huang et al.
summary: This paper introduces AdvancedMathBench, a benchmark for evaluating how well large language models can generate and verify advanced mathematical proofs at the undergraduate and doctoral level. Current models struggle significantly on this task — even the best achieves only 66% on doctoral-level problems — and the paper also shows that models are poor at detecting errors in proofs, suggesting that reasoning verification remains a critical unsolved problem.

title: Input-Aware Dynamic Backdoor Attack Against Quantum Neural Networks
authors: Junrui Zhang, Zemin Chen, Lusi Li, Mohammad Ghasemigol, Daniel Takabi et al.
summary: This paper presents Q-DIBA, the first input-aware dynamic backdoor attack against Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) — machine learning models that run on quantum computers. Unlike previous quantum backdoor attacks that use fixed triggers, Q-DIBA adapts the attack trigger to each input, making it harder to detect while maintaining the QNN's normal performance on clean data. This matters because QNNs are emerging as practical quantum machine learning tools, and understanding their security vulnerabilities is critical before they're deployed in sensitive applications.

title: LoRA-Based Cascaded Multimodal Fusion for Action Recognition in Medical Training Environments
authors: Divya Mereddy, Jeevan Beedareddy
summary: This paper proposes a parameter-efficient method for recognizing actions and activities in medical training videos by fusing multiple data streams (video, skeleton pose, RGB frames, etc.) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Instead of retraining an entire model when adding new modalities, the framework adapts modalities in stages, starting with closely related ones and progressively adding others. Testing on nursing training datasets shows the approach works well and scales efficiently across different combinations of input data.

title: Transformer-Guided Swarm Intelligence for Frugal Neural Architecture Search
authors: Romain Amigon
summary: This paper presents a hybrid Neural Architecture Search (NAS) method that combines Transformers and swarm intelligence to design efficient deep learning models on consumer-grade hardware instead of requiring thousands of GPU-days. The approach discovers compact, high-performing architectures by pairing a Transformer-based RL controller for broad exploration with an Artificial Bee Colony algorithm for local refinement, achieving competitive accuracy on CIFAR-10 with drastically fewer parameters and in just 3 hours of search time.

title: Forgetting Our Way to Shared Meaning: Effects of Forgetting on Conceptual Alignment in a Non-Partnership Coordination Game
authors: Landon Liu, Mary Kelly, Alan Tsang
summary: This paper investigates how memory and forgetting affect whether multiple agents can reach shared understanding of concepts through coordination. The authors model conceptual alignment as a non-partnership game (where agents don't have identical goals) and show that adaptive agents converge faster to agreement, while memory degradation—counterintuitively—can stabilize agreed-upon meanings. This matters because it reveals that perfect memory isn't necessary for language coordination, and that perceived convergence can diverge from actual agreement.

title: How Temperature Shapes Ideological Discourse in Retrieval-Augmented Generation?
authors: Elmira Salari, Hazem Amamou, José Victor de Souza, Shruti Kshirsagar, Maria Nunes Delfino et al.
summary: This paper investigates how ideological biases in retrieved documents affect the outputs of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems — a technique where LLMs are grounded by external documents to reduce hallucinations. The authors find that RAG systems can transmit and amplify ideological positions from source materials, and that this effect varies significantly with sampling temperature, a parameter controlling randomness in text generation.

title: From Expressivity to Sample Complexity: Narrow Teachers for Transformers via C-RASP
authors: Michael Rizvi-Martel, Satwik Bhattamishra, Guillaume Rabusseau, Michael Hahn
summary: This paper bridges a gap in transformer theory: while prior work showed *what* tasks transformers can express, this work tackles *how efficiently* they learn those tasks. The authors propose sample complexity bounds for learning C-RASP constructions (a formal language for describing transformer computations), moving from expressivity questions toward practical learnability guarantees.

title: Paradoxes of Game Theoretic Equilibria and Price of Anarchy
authors: Georgios Piliouras, Ian Gemp, Siqi Liu, Luke Marris
summary: This paper exposes fundamental flaws in how game theorists evaluate multi-agent learning and efficiency. The authors show that standard solution concepts (Nash equilibria, Price of Anarchy) rest on mathematically unstable foundations—agents converging to these "equilibria" may exhibit chaotic behavior, and worst-case efficiency guarantees collapse when costs vary even slightly. The work challenges decades of algorithmic game theory orthodoxy by proving that dynamic learning trajectories diverge sharply from static equilibrium predictions.

title: When Local Monitors Miss Compositional Harm: Diagnosing Distributed Backdoors in Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Yibo Hu, Ren Wang
summary: This paper reveals a critical vulnerability in runtime safety monitors for multi-agent LLM systems: attackers can split harmful payloads across multiple agents so each individual message passes safety checks, but the combined result is malicious. The authors prove that once attack fragments appear benign to a monitor's view, no detector using that view can catch them — no matter how strong — unless the monitor can see the assembled payload in its final representation.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for C-V2X RAT Selection
authors: Moritz Schaffenroth, Uwe Kölbel, Heike Lepke, Alexander Prinz, Alfred Höß
summary: This paper applies multi-agent reinforcement learning (specifically MAPPO, a distributed variant) to the problem of choosing which communication technology vehicles should use in real-time: cellular networks, direct V2X sidelinks, or both. The work matters because connected vehicles need to balance conflicting demands (latency, reliability, bandwidth) across multiple applications, and the paper shows that learning-based channel selection outperforms static rules and single-agent deep RL approaches by 2–3% in delivery reliability while halving training time.

title: STEP: Career-Path Recommendation via Temporal and Educational Trajectory Modeling
authors: Iman Johary, Guillaume Bied, Alexandru C. Mara, Tijl De Bie
summary: This paper presents STEP, a system that predicts a person's next job by analyzing their career trajectory from resume data. It combines language models to extract structured career information from unstructured resumes with neural sequence models that learn temporal and educational patterns. The work enables large-scale career recommendation and labor market analysis, which matters for workforce planning, policy-making, and job-matching platforms.

title: JobHop v2: A Large-Scale Career Trajectory Dataset from Unstructured Resumes
authors: Iman Johary, Guillaume Bied, Alexandru C. Mara, Tijl De Bie
summary: JobHop v2 is a large-scale dataset of 355,000+ career trajectories extracted from real resumes using language models. The dataset fills a critical gap in workforce analytics by providing authentic, richly annotated career data at scale — something previously unavailable to independent researchers — enabling better job recommendation systems, labor market analysis, and career planning tools.

title: An Explainable Agentic System for Detection of Conversational Scams with Summary-Based Memory
authors: Ahmed Omar Salim Adnan, Yogananda Manjunath, Shivanjali Khare
summary: This paper presents an AI system that detects sophisticated scams conducted over many messages (like romance scams) by analyzing entire conversations rather than isolated texts. Unlike existing systems that flag individual phishing messages, this approach tracks how scammers gradually build trust and manipulate victims over weeks or months—and it explains its decisions to users, reaching 97.8% accuracy while helping people become more confident in identifying real scams.

title: Markov Chain Monte Carlo with Diffusion Paths
authors: Han Chen, Sifan Liu, Jun Yang
summary: This paper proposes MAD-Path, a new sampling method for drawing from complex multimodal distributions (distributions with multiple peaks). Instead of the classical tempering approach used in Markov chain Monte Carlo, the authors use diffusion paths—smooth mathematical trajectories from a simple Gaussian distribution to the target—and combine them with a correction mechanism to eliminate sampling bias. The method is important because it better preserves the relative importance of different modes and mixes faster, which matters for Bayesian inference and other applications where you need reliable samples from complicated posterior distributions.

title: Auditing the Risk Claims of Distributional Reinforcement Learning
authors: Hari Prasad
summary: This paper audits whether distributional reinforcement learning agents—which learn to predict full return distributions rather than just expected values—actually produce reliable risk estimates. Using rigorous statistical testing on trained agents (QR-DQN, C51, IQN), the authors find that 40–95% of claimed risk trade-offs are false; the learned distributions reflect training artifacts, not real environment uncertainty. This matters because practitioners increasingly trust these agents for safety-critical decisions.

title: DAG-FM: A Foundation Model for Causal Discovery under Heterogeneous Causal Mechanisms
authors: Yikang Chen, Zhengkang Guan, Haoyuan Qian, Peng Cui, Yi Yang et al.
summary: This paper introduces DAG-FM, a foundation model that discovers causal relationships in tabular data by learning to infer the structure of Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs — networks showing which variables cause which). The key innovation is handling *heterogeneous causal mechanisms* — real-world datasets where different variables follow different causal rules — using a mixture-of-experts approach that lets the model adapt to unknown assumptions about how causes produce effects.

title: CDFM: Towards a General-Purpose Causal Discovery Foundation Model
authors: Jie Qiao, Ruichu Cai, Zijian Li, Weilin Chen, Pengfei Hua et al.
summary: This paper proposes CDFM, a foundation model that learns to infer causal structures from data in a general-purpose way, rather than using specialized algorithms for each dataset type. The key insight is that by pretraining on diverse synthetic causal models, the model can discover causal relationships in new domains without task-specific tuning—a major shift from traditional causal discovery methods.

title: Event-based Neural Decoding for Neuroprosthetic Motor Control
authors: Khaleelulla Khan Nazeer, Sirine Arfa, Matthias Jobst, Richard George, Christian Mayr
summary: This paper presents a neural decoding system for prosthetic limb control that uses event-based spiking neural networks to reduce power consumption and communication latency. The key innovation is an event-based gated recurrent unit that generates sparse, graded spikes—enabling better task performance than traditional spiking networks while maintaining efficiency suitable for on-device processing in prosthetics.

title: Long-Memory Reservoir Computing for Data-Scarce Dengue Forecasting
authors: Rahul Goswami, Shinjini Paul, Palash Ghosh, Tanujit Chakraborty
summary: This paper tackles dengue forecasting in settings where data is scarce and messy—short, noisy, non-stationary time series with long-range memory. The authors combine reservoir computing (a lightweight neural network approach) with techniques from statistical time-series modeling to create forecasts that work well even with little training data. They propose two variants that explicitly capture long-term dependencies and provide uncertainty estimates, outperforming standard statistical and deep-learning methods on real dengue datasets.

title: Efficient and Robust Spiking Neural Networks for sEMG-Based Muscle Fatigue Detection
authors: Kaiwen Tang, Jiaqi Dong, Zhanglu Yan, Weng-Fai Wong
summary: This paper develops Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)—a brain-inspired neural architecture that processes information as sparse electrical spikes—to detect muscle fatigue from surface electromyography (sEMG) signals in wearable devices. The key innovation is a quantization-compatible training scheme that makes these networks robust to real-world noise while consuming 200x less energy than standard deep learning models, making them practical for always-on health monitoring.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Execution under Dynamic Fees in a Closed-Loop DEX Simulator
authors: Wen-Ting Wang
summary: This paper studies how traders should execute orders in automated market makers (AMMs) when the platform charges dynamic fees that vary with market conditions. Because real historical data can't show how traders would respond to fee changes that don't actually exist in the past, the authors built a realistic simulator with dynamic fees, multiple liquidity pools, and arbitrage logic, then trained a deep reinforcement learning agent (DQN) to find optimal execution strategies. The DQN agent outperforms hand-tuned baseline strategies by reducing execution costs, especially when fees are dynamic.

title: LayerNorm as Implicit Gain Control in Looped Transformers
authors: Matthias M. M. Buehlmaier
summary: This paper explains how LayerNorm functions as a stability mechanism in recurrent transformers by implicitly controlling gain — the strength of signal amplification through layers. The key finding is that LayerNorm decouples the stability constraint from the operator norm, allowing recurrent blocks to remain stable even when their largest singular value exceeds 1, provided the spectral margin (a tighter measure) stays within bounds. This work bridges architectural design and dynamical systems theory.

title: Emergent Generalization by Representation Learning in Artificial Neural Networks
authors: Hardik Rajpal, Dan Goodman
summary: This paper shows that artificial neural networks learn to generalize better when forced to compress information into low-dimensional representations — a discovery backed by information theory and validated against real brain recordings from mice. The work bridges neuroscience and machine learning by demonstrating that compact learned representations aren't just a byproduct of neural activity, but actively enable networks to make accurate predictions on unseen data.

title: A Symbolic Neural CPU for Quantization-Simulated Writeback and Interpretable Program Execution
authors: Jose Luis Lima de Jesus Silva
summary: This paper presents a neural network architecture that can execute algorithmic programs while exposing its internal decision-making process at every step. The key innovation is making neural execution "traceable" — you can see which operation was selected, which registers were read and written, and how memory changed — while maintaining correctness even under 8-bit quantization. This matters because neural networks that execute algorithms are usually black boxes; this work opens them up to inspection.

title: PHINN-EEG: Topological Time-Series Analysis of Dream-State EEG -- Dynamic Betti Curves for Dream Content Classification and Topology-Conditioned Neural Signal Synthesis
authors: Ren Takahashi, Emre Yusuf, Jayabrata Bhaduri
summary: This paper introduces PHINN-EEG, a novel approach to detecting dream states from EEG recordings using topological data analysis instead of traditional power spectral features. Rather than measuring just the energy of brain signals, the method extracts "Dynamic Betti Curves"—mathematical descriptors of the geometric shape of neural activity in high-dimensional space—and combines them with a generative model to both classify dreams and synthesize realistic EEG data. The work claims to improve classification accuracy from ~70% to 82–90% on a large open-access dataset.

title: Scalable Visual Pretraining for Language Intelligence
authors: Yiming Zhang, Zhonghan Zhao, Wenwei Zhang, Haiteng Zhao, Tianyang Lin et al.
summary: This paper challenges the standard practice of converting visually rich documents (PDFs, web pages, scientific papers) into plain text before training language models. Instead, the authors show that training directly on visual representations—preserving figures, equations, and layouts—produces better language models more efficiently. This matters because documents contain information that text extraction inevitably loses, and the paper demonstrates this isn't just theoretically interesting but practically beneficial at scale.

title: Evolution of Accuracy and Visual-Cognitive Errors in a Decade of Vision-Language AI Models
authors: Shravan Murlidaran, Miguel P. Eckstein
summary: This paper tracks how vision-language models (VLMs)—AI systems that understand both images and text—have improved over a decade (2017–2025) by evaluating them on complex social scenes rather than simple benchmark datasets. The authors introduce a new dataset of 100 images showing complex human interactions and categorize the types of errors these models make, finding that modern multimodal large language models (MLLMs) now match human-level performance on scene description except for differences in which image regions they attend to.

title: VEXAIoT: Autonomous IoT Vulnerability EXploitation using AI Agents
authors: Katherine Swinea, Kshitiz Aryal, Lopamudra Praharaj, Maanak Gupta
summary: VEXAIoT is an autonomous framework that uses AI agents powered by large language models to discover and exploit vulnerabilities in IoT devices. The system combines a detection agent and an attack execution agent to perform reconnaissance, plan exploits, and execute attacks against vulnerable IoT services, achieving a 95% success rate across 260 test scenarios in controlled environments.

title: Deep Gaussian Processes on Directed Acyclic Graphs
authors: Federico L. Perlino, Oliver Hamelijnck, Adam M. Johansen, Theodoros Damoulas
summary: This paper develops Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) — probabilistic models that stack multiple layers of uncertainty — specifically designed to work on directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) where functions compose along edges and observations are scattered throughout the structure. The work matters because many real systems (causal mechanisms, multi-fidelity simulations, gene networks) have this DAG structure, and existing methods struggle with uncertainty propagation through such compositional systems while handling noisy, irregularly-sampled data.

title: Semantic Pareto-DQN: A Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning Framework for Financial Anomaly Detection
authors: Cláudio Lúcio do Val Lopes, Lucca Machado da Silva
summary: This paper tackles financial fraud detection by combining reinforcement learning with LLM-generated semantic representations to handle the "fraud collapse" problem—where imbalanced datasets cause systems to ignore rare fraud cases. The key insight is treating fraud detection as a multi-objective optimization problem where an RL agent navigates trade-offs between catching fraud, minimizing false alarms, and discovering new anomaly patterns without needing artificial data resampling.

title: Task-Specific Multimodal Question Answering Agents via Confidence Calibration and Incremental Reasoning for QANTA 2026
authors: Nirjhar Das, Md. Al-Mamun Provath
summary: This paper presents a two-agent system for multimodal question answering in the QANTA 2026 competition, where systems must answer trivia questions from incrementally revealed text and images under efficiency constraints. The key innovation is using task-specific reasoning strategies and confidence calibration rather than retrieval pipelines or ensembles — one lightweight agent for deciding *when* to answer (Tossup), another for selecting *correct* answers (Bonus) — achieving top leaderboard performance. The work shows that simple, well-designed reasoning policies can outperform more complex approaches on resource-constrained multimodal tasks.

title: LLM for EDA in Front-End Design: Challenges and Opportunities
authors: Kangwei Xu, Bing Li, Ulf Schlichtmann
summary: This paper surveys how Large Language Models (LLMs) can transform electronic design automation (EDA) — the software tools that help engineers design semiconductor chips. The authors argue that LLMs have moved beyond simple assistance to enable fully autonomous agents that can generate hardware code, create test cases, and explore design alternatives end-to-end, potentially solving a major bottleneck in chip design cycles.

title: Dynamic Frechet Regression with Feature Selection for Distributional Data
authors: Kiran Adhikari, Amrutha Dinesh, Mathew Kuttolamadom, Ying Lin
summary: This paper introduces Dynamic Fréchet Regression (DFR), a method for predicting probability distributions that change over time or along some ordered dimension (like depth in a material). Instead of predicting single numbers, DFR predicts entire distributions while identifying which input variables matter most — a capability important for scientific fields like manufacturing where uncertainty itself is the outcome of interest.

title: Mosaic: Runtime-Efficient Multi-Agent Embodied Planning
authors: Kunjal Panchal, Saayan Mitra, Sunav Choudhary, Victor Bursztyn, Somdeb Sarkhel et al.
summary: This paper tackles a critical bottleneck in LLM-based multi-agent planning: the high latency caused by failed actions and poor coordination. Mosaic proposes a framework combining lightweight semantic memory (storing objects in relative coordinates) with Integer Linear Programming for constraint-based coordination, achieving 27-32% faster execution and higher success rates on embodied AI tasks. This matters because it makes multi-agent LLM planning practical for real-time applications.

title: Agora: Enhancing LLM Agent Reasoning Via Auction-Based Task Allocation
authors: Kaiji Zhou, Ales Leonardis, Yue Feng
summary: This paper presents Agora, a framework that uses auction mechanisms to dynamically route reasoning tasks to the most suitable expert models and tools within an LLM agent system. Rather than matching tasks to experts based solely on functional similarity, Agora allocates work based on each expert's actual competence and cost efficiency, improving reasoning quality and controllable cost-benefit trade-offs across multiple benchmarks.

title: A censoring-aware target interface for tabular foundation models in survival prediction
authors: Yue Lyu, Steven H. Lin, Xuelin Huang, Ziyi Li
summary: This paper solves a fundamental problem in medical AI: how to use tabular foundation models (reusable neural networks trained on diverse datasets) for survival prediction when patient follow-up data is incomplete. The key innovation is converting censored survival outcomes into pseudo-observations based on restricted mean survival time (RMST), allowing standard regression models to handle incomplete data without survival-specific training. The approach is tested on 36 real medical datasets and shows competitive performance with traditional survival methods.

title: The Count Is There, but Misaligned: Understanding and Correcting Counting Failures in VLMs
authors: Ahmed Oumar El-Shangiti, Abzal Nurgazy, Hilal AlQuabeh, Nikolai Rozanov, Kentaro Inui
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with basic object counting despite strong performance on other tasks. This paper discovers that VLMs often internally represent the correct count but output wrong answers — a misalignment between internal knowledge and external behavior. The authors propose a simple fix: an error detector that identifies when the model is likely to fail, then triggers a re-prompt, improving counting accuracy by up to 15.6 percentage points with no retraining.

title: CoCoT-EEG: Contrastive-Pretrained Multiscale Convolutional Transformer for EEG Decoding
authors: Gabriel Mahuas, Victoria Shevchenko, Ugo Tanielian, Yassir Bendou, Richard Gao
summary: This paper presents CoCoT, a self-supervised foundation model for decoding brain activity from EEG (electroencephalogram) signals. Rather than using the standard approach of masking and reconstructing raw EEG data, the authors use contrastive learning — a technique that learns by comparing similar and dissimilar examples — combined with multiscale convolutional layers and Transformer blocks. The model outperforms previous EEG decoders on multiple benchmark tasks and works well even without pretraining, suggesting that the architecture itself is particularly suited to noisy, information-sparse neurophysiological signals.

title: Statistically Undetectable Backdoors in Deep Neural Networks
authors: Andrej Bogdanov, Alon Rosen, Neekon Vafa
summary: This paper demonstrates that adversaries can plant hidden "backdoors" in deep neural networks that are mathematically indistinguishable from honestly trained models, even when inspecting all the weights. These backdoors enable attackers to manipulate the model's behavior on specific inputs while remaining invisible to standard detection methods. The work reveals a fundamental asymmetry: the attacker who plants the backdoor can easily exploit it, but defenders cannot detect or prevent it under realistic assumptions.

title: FreyaTTS Technical Report
authors: Ahmet Erdem Pamuk, Ömer Yentür, Ahmet Tunga Bayrak, Yavuz Alp Sencer Öztürk, Mustafa Yavuz
summary: Freya-TTS is a compact 183M-parameter text-to-speech model optimized for Turkish, using a diffusion-based approach that works directly from text without requiring phoneme conversion or discrete speech tokenizers. It achieves high-quality 48 kHz audio synthesis while running faster than real-time on consumer CPUs, making it practical for edge deployment.

title: Shared Selective Persistent Memory for Agentic LLM Systems
authors: Sanjana Pedada, Aditya Dhavala, Neelraj Patil
summary: This paper tackles a practical problem in LLM-based agent systems: when agents perform multi-turn tasks (like generating code or dashboards), they traditionally lose all learned context between sessions, forcing them to re-specify the same configurations, schemas, and constraints repeatedly. The authors propose "shared selective persistent memory"—a system that intelligently saves only reusable context (task specs, data schemas, tool configs, output rules) while discarding session-specific reasoning, and crucially, allows this memory to be shared across users with access controls. In a deployed platform, this achieves 96% task completion versus 79% without memory, while cutting token costs by 97x and task time by 14x through a "zero-token refresh" mechanism that decouples data from generated code.

title: Foveation-Guided Dynamic Token Selection for Robust and Efficient Vision Transformers
authors: Ibrahim Batuhan Akkaya, Kishaan Jeeveswaran, Bahram Zonooz, Elahe Arani
summary: This paper introduces Foveated Dynamic Transformer (FDT), a vision transformer that mimics how the human eye focuses attention on important image regions while ignoring irrelevant details. The key innovation is a "foveation-guided" token selection mechanism that reduces computational cost by 34% while improving accuracy and robustness against noise and adversarial attacks—without any explicit adversarial training.

title: Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility Using First-order Conditions
authors: Xianhua Peng, Wu Guo, Songyan Wang, Jianfei Zhu
summary: This paper introduces CEFOL (Certainty-Equivalent First-Order Learning), a deep learning algorithm for solving dynamic programming problems where agents have recursive preferences (preferences that depend on both immediate and future consumption in a nonlinear way). The key innovation is using neural networks to represent the certainty equivalent — a mathematical object that appears in the optimality conditions but is expensive to compute — allowing the algorithm to directly enforce first-order optimality conditions rather than just approximating value functions. The method achieves high precision on realistic economic problems like consumption-saving decisions and macroeconomic models.

title: Test-Time Scaling for Small VLMs on Multilingual Visual MCQ
authors: Spiros Baxevanakis, Peng-Jian Yang
summary: This paper investigates whether test-time scaling (TTS) — using extra compute at inference time to improve reasoning — works for small vision-language models on multilingual visual multiple-choice questions. The authors find that success depends far less on sophisticated search algorithms than on basic factors: whether the model can actually finish its reasoning and commit to an answer. Their best approach reaches 84.1% accuracy on a leaderboard by focusing on parseability and decoding budget rather than elaborate verification machinery.

title: A Sovereign, Open-Source Foundation Model for German and English
authors: The Soofi-Team,  :, Benedikt Droste, David Fitzek, Ruben Härle et al.
summary: Soofi S is an open-source foundation model (a pre-trained neural network serving as a base for downstream AI tasks) optimized for German and English, built using a hybrid Mixture-of-Experts Mamba-Transformer architecture. The key innovation is efficiency: it only activates 3 billion of its 30 billion parameters per input token and maintains constant memory usage as context length grows, making it significantly faster and cheaper to deploy than conventional dense models while matching or exceeding the performance of much larger competitors on standardized benchmarks.

title: Similarity search generalisation in contrastive learning with InfoNCE loss
authors: Nick Whiteley
summary: This paper provides theoretical guarantees for contrastive learning with InfoNCE loss, showing how similarity search performance generalizes from training to unseen data. The key insight is that as you increase the number of negative samples (k), the learned embeddings approximate an idealized similarity search up to an error of O(1/k), and the authors introduce a new continuity bound that explains why using more negatives stabilizes learning.

title: Communication-Efficient Digital-Twin Coordination for Heterogeneous LLM Embodied Agents over Computing Power Networks
authors: Nuocheng Yang, Sihua Wang, Zihan Chen, Tony Q. S. Quek, Changchuan Yin
summary: This paper proposes LDT-Coord, a framework for coordinating teams of robots or autonomous agents powered by different large language models (LLMs) while minimizing communication overhead. Instead of having agents negotiate through natural-language dialogue, the framework uses a lightweight "digital twin" server that collects structured action reports and conflict constraints from each agent, then uses a rule-based algorithm to resolve conflicts and issue coordination instructions. The approach reduces communication by over 70x while maintaining task success rates comparable to traditional methods, even when agents have heterogeneous (unequal) LLM capabilities.

title: When is Routing Meaningful? Diversity and Robustness in Language Model Societies
authors: Fantine Huot, Michael Kaisers, Mirella Lapata
summary: This paper argues that routing policies in multi-model LLM systems are typically evaluated only on accuracy and cost, missing two critical properties: whether the models actually behave differently (behavioral differentiation) and whether the routing is stable under input variations (robustness). The authors show that high accuracy can mask meaningless routing—where either all models respond identically or the same query gets routed inconsistently—and propose metrics to diagnose these failure modes.

title: Control Laguerre Tessellation: Semi-discrete Optimal Transport Over Control Systems
authors: Ripon C. Sarker, Abhishek Halder
summary: This paper extends optimal transport theory — the mathematical framework for efficiently moving probability mass from one distribution to another — to systems where agents move under control constraints (like robots or vehicles with limited acceleration). The key insight is that when transportation costs reflect the optimal control cost of agent motion, the solution has a clean geometric structure called a Laguerre tessellation (a partition of space based on distances to target points). The paper demonstrates this for linear systems with energy and time objectives, bridging control theory and optimal transport.

title: Augmenting Fundamental Analysis with Large Language Models: A RAG-Based System for Generating Investor Briefs
authors: Bartosz Ziółko, Kacper Dobrzeniewski
summary: This paper demonstrates how to use large language models (LLMs) combined with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to automatically generate investment briefs from company reports, macroeconomic data, and SEC filings. The authors built a working system using GPT-4o, tracked 9 companies over 4 weeks, and validated the approach with real individual investors—showing that LLMs can meaningfully assist fundamental analysis, which traditionally relies on manual document review and interpretation.

title: Stochastic Linear Bandits with Partially Observed Actions
authors: Gautam Dasarathy, Vineet Gattani, Lalit Jain
summary: This paper studies stochastic linear bandits — a fundamental sequential decision-making problem where an agent must learn which actions yield the best rewards — under a realistic constraint: the agent only observes partial information about each action. The key innovation is showing that when actions live in a low-dimensional subspace (intrinsic dimension), an algorithm called TOFU-POV can achieve near-optimal learning rates despite this missing information, solving a problem that was previously thought impossible in the general case.

title: Herding and Liquidity in Order-Book Markets. I. A Robust Liquidity-Stress Crossover and its Reflexive Mechanism
authors: Jan Novotny
summary: This paper uses agent-based modeling to study when order-book markets transition from liquid to stressed—specifically, when one side of the market dries up. The authors combine zero-intelligence traders (who follow fundamentals) with chartist herders (who follow price momentum), and carefully map out the conditions under which instability emerges. The key finding is a robust, smooth "liquidity-stress crossover" driven by herding feedback, where buying momentum begets more buying, creating self-reinforcing imbalances.

title: UniClawBench: A Universal Benchmark for Proactive Agents on Real-World Tasks
authors: Zhekai Chen, Chengqi Duan, Kaiyue Sun, Bohao Li, Yuqing Wang et al.
summary: UniClawBench is the first benchmark designed to evaluate proactive AI agents (systems that use large language models to autonomously accomplish real-world tasks) in realistic, dynamic environments rather than static sandboxed ones. The benchmark measures five core agent capabilities—skill usage, exploration, reasoning with long documents, understanding images/multimodal content, and coordinating across multiple platforms—using 400 real-world tasks evaluated in live Docker containers with step-by-step progress tracking. This matters because existing benchmarks poorly capture whether agents can actually help users in messy, real-world settings where tasks span multiple steps, require tool use, and involve human feedback loops.

title: SLORR: Simple and Efficient In-Training Low-Rank Regularization
authors: David González-Martínez, Shiwei Liu
summary: This paper introduces SLORR, a method to make neural networks easier to compress without sacrificing accuracy. The key innovation is a regularization technique applied *during training* that encourages weight matrices to have low-rank structure — meaning they can be factorized into smaller, simpler matrices. Unlike prior approaches, SLORR is simple to implement, doesn't modify the network architecture, and adds minimal computational overhead (under 1-8%), making it practical for both vision models and large language models.

title: AUTOPILOT VQA: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for Incident-Centric Dashcam Understanding
authors: Siddharth Damodharan, Radhika Gupta, Ali Alshami, Ryan Rabinowitz, Jugal Kalita
summary: This paper introduces AUTOPILOT-VQA, a benchmark dataset that tests how well vision-language models (neural networks that process both images and text) can understand and reason about driving incidents from dashcam video. The work addresses a critical gap: while these models excel at simple object recognition, they struggle with safety-critical reasoning needed for autonomous driving—judging whether an accident was avoidable, identifying what caused it, or assessing road conditions. The benchmark matters because autonomous vehicles need to be evaluated on real-world safety scenarios, not just generic image understanding tasks.

title: ARDY: Autoregressive Diffusion with Hybrid Representation for Interactive Human Motion Generation
authors: Kaifeng Zhao, Mathis Petrovich, Haotian Zhang, Tingwu Wang, Siyu Tang et al.
summary: ARDY is a real-time motion generation system that creates realistic 3D human animations from text prompts and kinematic constraints. It solves a practical problem: existing methods are either slow but precise (offline) or fast but inflexible (online). ARDY achieves both by using a hybrid neural representation and a streaming autoregressive architecture, making it suitable for interactive applications like video games, VR, and robotics.

title: Workflow as Knowledge: Semantic Persistence for LLM-Mediated Workflows
authors: Emanuele Quinto, Carlo Andrea Rozzi, Francesco Zanitti
summary: This paper proposes a conceptual framework for representing LLM-mediated workflows as persistent, inspectable knowledge objects rather than ephemeral execution traces. The key insight is treating workflow definitions, instances, inference records, and dependencies as first-class entities in a shared knowledge substrate—enabling workflows to be resumed, reviewed, and reasoned about as structured data. This matters because current workflow systems treat LLM calls as black boxes; making workflows themselves transparent and persistent enables better debugging, auditability, and human oversight of LLM applications.

title: The Illusion of Equivalency: Statistical Characterization of Quantization Effects in LLMs
authors: Baha Rababah, Cuneyt Gurcan Akcora, Carson K. Leung
summary: This paper challenges the conventional wisdom that quantization (compressing model weights to fewer bits) preserves model behavior when standard metrics like accuracy stay high. The authors show that quantized LLMs actually make different predictions than their full-precision counterparts on individual examples, even when overall performance appears identical—what they call an "illusion of equivalence." They propose new evaluation methods and analyze where in the model structure quantization causes the most damage.

title: Pose-to-Biomechanics: Bridging 3D Human Pose Estimation and Biomechanical Attribute Prediction
authors: Ayda Eghbalian, Kevin Desai
summary: This paper introduces BioModule, a lightweight neural network module that translates 3D skeleton poses (from video-based pose estimators) into biomechanical measurements like joint forces, muscle activations, and movement efficiency. The work bridges computer vision and biomechanics by creating a large aligned dataset and showing that existing pose estimators can be retrofitted to provide clinically and scientifically meaningful motion analysis without modification.

title: Latent Memory Palace: Reasoning for Control as Autoregressive Variational Inference
authors: Chuning Zhu, Eva Xu, Jose Barreiros, Krishnan Srinivasan, Paarth Shah et al.
summary: This paper introduces Latent Memory Palace (LMP), a method that enables control policies (robots, agents) to "reason" by organizing information in a learned latent space, similar to how language models solve hard problems through step-by-step thinking. Unlike prior work that reasons in language space, LMP reasons in a continuous latent space tailored for spatial and motor tasks, using variational inference to balance deliberation depth with immediate action. The method works in both simulation and real robots, and also produces a better tokenizer for downstream policies.

title: Remember When It Matters: Proactive Memory Agent for Long-Horizon Agents
authors: Yifan Wu, Lizhu Zhang, Yuhang Zhou, Mingyi Wang, Bo Peng et al.
summary: This paper addresses a critical failure mode in long-horizon AI agents: as task trajectories grow, decision-relevant information gets buried or forgotten, causing performance to decay. The authors propose a "memory agent" — a separate module that runs alongside the main action agent, actively deciding when to remind it of important past context rather than passively storing information. The approach is plug-and-play and shows consistent improvements (+6–8 percentage points) across multiple benchmarks.

title: LTM: Large-scale Terrain Model for Wildfire-prone Landscapes
authors: Xiao Fu, Yue Hu, Meida Chen, Peter Anthony Beerel, Barath Raghavan
summary: This paper proposes a method to quickly and cheaply reconstruct high-resolution 3D terrain maps from aerial images in wildfire-prone regions by using outdated terrain data (Digital Elevation Models) as geometric priors. Rather than expensively matching features between images, the method aligns pixels directly to the existing terrain map using physics-based constraints, achieving real-time performance while improving accuracy over existing approaches.

title: MPFlow: Learning Budgeted Max-Flow Optimization on the Lightning Network with Deep Graph Reinforcement Learning
authors: Harrison Rush, Vincent Davis, Simone Antonelli, Vikash Singh, Jesse Shrader et al.
summary: This paper solves a real optimization problem in Bitcoin's Lightning Network: given a fixed budget, which payment channels should a node open to maximize its routing capacity? The authors use deep reinforcement learning with graph neural networks to learn where to place liquidity, and their system is already deployed in production managing millions of dollars in cryptocurrency. The key insight is using max-flow as a principled objective and training the AI to avoid simply connecting to network hubs—which would be the obvious but suboptimal strategy.

title: Do You Need a Frontier Model as a Citation Verifier? Benchmarking Rubric LLMs for Deep-Research Source Attribution
authors: Ethan Leung, Elias Lumer, Corey Feld, Austin Huber, Vamse Kumar Subbiah et al.
summary: This paper investigates whether expensive frontier LLMs are necessary to judge citation quality in AI research systems—or whether cheaper models work just as well. The authors benchmark 8 LLMs as "judges" that evaluate whether citations support claims, finding that smaller models like GPT-4o-mini perform competitively, but all models show hidden biases (false positive/negative rates) that scalar metrics like F1 scores hide. This matters because these judges are used as reward signals in reinforcement learning training, so their hidden biases get baked into the AI system itself.

title: ProjAgent: Procedural Similarity Retrieval for Repository-Level Code Generation
authors: QiHong Chen, Aaron Imani, Iftekhar Ahmed
summary: This paper presents ProjAgent, a system for generating code at the repository level by retrieving similar functions based on procedural logic rather than just lexical or semantic similarity. The key innovation is using an "agentic workflow" to decompose target functions into reasoning steps, then finding existing repository functions that solve similar procedural problems—even if they use different variable names or operate in different domains. The system combines this procedural retrieval with conventional semantic search and iterative code repair, achieving strong results on a standard benchmark.

title: SolarChain-Eval: A Physics-Constrained Benchmark for Trustworthy Economic Agents in Decentralized Energy Markets
authors: Shilin Ou, Yifan Xu, Luyao Zhang
summary: This paper introduces SolarChain-Eval, a benchmark for testing whether autonomous agents can safely and fairly operate in decentralized energy markets. The key insight is that agents optimizing for profit alone may exploit physical constraints (like falsifying solar generation data) or create market instability — so evaluation must measure both economic performance and safety, with transparent oversight from an LLM-based auditor that logs all high-risk decisions.

title: Resample or Reroute? Budget-Aware Test-Time Model Selection for Large Language Models
authors: Teng-Ruei Chen
summary: This paper addresses a practical problem in deploying multiple large language models (LLMs): how to best spend a limited per-query budget on either resampling the same model or rerouting to a different one. The authors propose an online policy that dynamically allocates budget between these two strategies using an imperfect verifier, and show it achieves better cost-quality tradeoffs than existing approaches across multiple benchmarks.

title: WebSwarm: Recursive Multi-Agent Orchestration for Deep-and-Wide Web Search
authors: Xiaoshuai Song, Liancheng Zhang, Kangzhi Zhao, Yutao Zhu, Zhongyuan Wang et al.
summary: WebSwarm is a framework that orchestrates multiple LLM-based agents working together recursively to perform complex web search tasks that require both depth (following chains of reasoning) and breadth (exploring multiple angles). Instead of using a single agent that gets stuck in long trajectories or multiple agents that only run in parallel, WebSwarm dynamically creates a tree of specialized search agents that delegate subtasks to each other and build on shared evidence, enabling more thorough and nuanced information gathering.

title: EdgeRefine: Privacy-Utility Balance for Graphs via Jaccard Sampling under Edge Differential Privacy
authors: Wenxiu Ding, Muzhi Liu, Zheng Yan, Mingjun Wang, Yifan Zhao et al.
summary: This paper tackles a fundamental problem in privacy-preserving graph learning: how to protect sensitive link information in graphs while keeping the model accurate enough to be useful. The authors propose EdgeRefine, a method that intelligently removes noisy edges added for privacy protection, achieving privacy levels comparable to prior work but with substantially better accuracy—up to 19.7% improvement on standard benchmarks.

title: Formal Mechanisms for Market Stability in Self-Interested Agent Societies: A Marketplace Simulation Study
authors: Eugene Ng Yi Sheng, Bingquan Shen
summary: This paper asks: what rules and mechanisms keep self-interested agents honest in a marketplace where they could defect for personal gain? The researchers simulated 18 LLM-based agents trading with each other and tested eight different governance mechanisms (like mediation, reputation systems, etc.) under increasing adversarial attack. They found that "Mediation"—having a neutral third party resolve disputes—performed best and remained stable even when attacked by sophisticated, AI-optimized trolls that reduced honest traders' profits by only 13.3%.

title: Multi-Modal, Multi-Environment Machine Teaching for Robust Reward Learning
authors: Ali Larian, Qian Lin, Chang Zong Wu, Daniel S. Brown
summary: This paper addresses a core challenge in deploying autonomous agents: learning reward functions that work reliably across different environments rather than just memorizing patterns from a single training scenario. The authors combine inverse reinforcement learning (learning objectives from human feedback) with a smart teaching algorithm that selects which environments to train in and what type of feedback to request, enabling agents to generalize their learned goals to new deployment contexts.

title: UltraX: Refining Pre-Training Data at Scale with Adaptive Programmatic Editing
authors: Xinlong Zhao, Dongsheng Liu, Hengyu Zhao, Zixuan Fu, Zheng Wang et al.
summary: UltraX is a system for automatically improving the quality of training data used to build large language models, at the scale of billions of text examples. Rather than just deleting bad data or rewriting it with another LLM, UltraX uses programmatic editing — converting original-refined text pairs into structured "programs" (sequences of insertion, deletion, modification operations) — to refine data reliably and efficiently. This matters because as we run out of new text to train on, the quality of existing data has become the main lever for improving LLMs.

title: BiSCo-LLM: Lookup-Free Binary Spherical Coding for Extreme Low-Bit Large Language Model Compression
authors: Yuantian Shao, Peisong Wang, Zhilei Liu, Chuangyi Li, Yuanteng Chen et al.
summary: BiSCo-LLM is a compression method that shrinks large language models to extreme low-bit weights (approaching 2 bits per parameter) without storing explicit codebooks. Instead of traditional quantization or vector quantization, it maps weights onto a hypersphere, binarizes them into compact codes, and uses residual encoding plus distillation to recover lost information. This matters because it solves a critical deployment bottleneck: memory, bandwidth, and storage constraints that prevent LLMs from running on edge devices or in bandwidth-limited settings.

title: Steering Neural Network Training through Interpretable Constraints Based on Partial Dependence
authors: Yann Claes, Pierre Geurts, Vân Anh Huynh-Thu
summary: This paper proposes a method to steer neural network training using partial dependence constraints—statistical tools that measure how a model's output changes with specific input features.
