Rather than just explaining what a trained model learned, the authors use domain knowledge about desired input-output relationships as constraints during training, producing models that are simultaneously more accurate, data-efficient, and interpretable. The approach is validated on regression tasks including time-series forecasting.

title: The complexities of patient-centred conversational artificial intelligence
authors: João Matos, Olivia Buege, Donny Cheung, Gary S. Collins, Paula Dhiman et al.
summary: This paper examines how real patients interact with health chatbots in ways that differ significantly from the simulated, idealized interactions typically used to develop and test these systems. The researchers analyzed over 2,000 actual patient-chatbot conversations, built a realistic patient simulator that captures emotional states and communication styles, and showed that how a patient expresses themselves can dramatically change whether an LLM correctly assesses medical urgency—suggesting that current chatbot evaluation practices may mask real-world performance problems and health disparities.

title: When Structured Sparse Autoencoders Learn Consistent Concepts Across Modalities
authors: Weiduo Liao, Yunqiao Yang, Ying Wei
summary: This paper proposes Structured Sparse Autoencoders (S²AE), a method to extract interpretable features from vision-language models that remain consistent across both visual and textual modalities. Standard sparse autoencoders produce fragmented, inconsistent concepts in images; S²AE fixes this by adding spatial and semantic structure constraints, achieving better semantic alignment and more interpretable, disentangled representations in multimodal models.

title: It Takes a MAESTRO To Prune Bad Experts
authors: Palaash Goel, Ayush Maheshwari, Tanmoy Chakraborty
summary: This paper addresses a critical deployment problem in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models: while these models are efficient during inference by activating only a subset of parameters per token, they still require storing all expert parameters in memory, creating a memory bottleneck. The authors propose MAESTRO, a pruning method that removes unimportant experts by modeling how experts are activated across layers using Markov chains, significantly improving compression while maintaining performance across diverse tasks.

title: Early to Share, Late to Save: Synchronisation-Driven Communication Gating in Bandwidth-Constrained Cooperative VLN
authors: Arav Gupta, Nivedan Yakolli, Avinash Gautam
summary: This paper tackles Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) — where embodied agents must follow natural language instructions to navigate physical environments — in settings with limited communication bandwidth. The authors propose "hindsight gating," a method that learns *when* agents should share information by analyzing which communication moments would have prevented navigation failures. They discover that agents should communicate early and confidently, not when uncertain, because early shared representations help both agents navigate the rest of the task more coherently.

title: Cross-seed explainability using Procrustes-conditioned Joint End-to-end Top-K Sparse Autoencoders
authors: Bendegúz Váradi, Zoltán Kmetty
summary: This paper solves a fundamental problem in neural network interpretability: when you train the same model architecture multiple times from different random starting points, the internal features it learns end up in different coordinate systems, making it impossible to compare what they've "learned." The authors develop a method using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and geometric alignment to extract universal, interpretable features that stay consistent across independently trained BERT models, validated on three text classification tasks.

title: Cognitive-structured Multimodal Agent for Multimodal Understanding, Generation, and Editing
authors: Feng Wang, Canmiao Fu, Zhipeng Huang, Chen Li, Jing Lyu et al.
summary: This paper proposes a Cognitive-structured Multimodal Agent that handles long-horizon conversations involving both images and text by storing visual information in an external memory rather than keeping everything in context. The agent selectively retrieves relevant past images during reasoning, enabling it to maintain coherent multimodal dialogue over 20+ turns while running 4× faster than baseline models and achieving higher accuracy—showing that structured memory beats naive parameter scaling for multimodal AI systems.

title: Ensemble Diversity Optimization for Subjective Supervision
authors: Xia Cui, Ziyi Huang, N. R. Abeynayake
summary: This paper tackles a real problem in NLP: when humans disagree about subjective tasks (e.g., is this text abusive?), standard models try to collapse that disagreement into a single prediction, losing important information. The authors propose Ensemble Diversity Optimization (EDO), a method that learns how to combine multiple models to preserve meaningful disagreement while staying well-calibrated (producing probability estimates that match reality). The work shows substantial improvements in uncertainty quantification across four subjective text-classification benchmarks.

title: Statistical Efficiency and Inference of Quantile Distributional Reinforcement Learning
authors: Zijie Cheng, Yang Peng, Zhihua Zhang
summary: This paper analyzes the statistical efficiency of quantile-based distributional reinforcement learning, focusing on policy evaluation—estimating the full distribution of cumulative rewards rather than just expected values. The authors prove that their quantile estimator achieves optimal sample complexity (√n convergence rate) and remains statistically efficient even as the number of quantiles grows, providing both finite-sample guarantees and asymptotic inference tools for principled decision-making in RL.

title: When Synthetic Speech Is All You Have: Better Call GRPO
authors: Shashi Kumar, Yanis Labrak, Hasindri Watawana, Sergio Burdisso, Esaú Villatoro-Tello et al.
summary: This paper shows how to adapt speech recognition models to specialized domains (like banking) when real speech data is scarce or legally restricted. Instead of using traditional supervised learning on synthetic speech from text-to-speech systems, the authors use reinforcement learning (specifically GRPO — a critic-free method) to squeeze much better performance from the same synthetic data, reducing errors by 40% compared to supervised approaches.

title: Beyond Backpropagation: Monte Carlo Method Can Train Deep Neural Networks
authors: Hong Zhao
summary: This paper demonstrates that a simple Monte Carlo method—randomly mutating parameters and keeping changes that reduce loss—can train deep neural networks without backpropagation or gradients. The work challenges the assumption that gradient-based optimization is necessary for deep learning, showing the method works on networks with 20+ layers, wide networks, and even Transformers, without requiring batch normalization or residual connections.

title: Prompt Compression via Activation Aggregation
authors: Thibaud Ardoin, Semira Einsele, Evis Bregu, Gerhard Wunder
summary: This paper shows that task-relevant information in instruction prompts can be compressed into a single activation vector—a learned weighted sum of hidden representations from a middle layer—and re-injected at an early layer with minimal accuracy loss (under 2%). The technique reduces computational cost for fixed instruction prompts and reveals how information flows across layers in large language models, suggesting that mid-layer representations are compatible with early-layer processing.

title: Token-Flow Firewall: Semantic Runtime Auditing for Persistent AI Agents
authors: Puji Wang, Yingchen Zhang, Ruqing Zhang, Jiafeng Guo, Xueqi Cheng
summary: This paper addresses security risks in persistent AI agents—long-lived systems that maintain memory and interact with tools over time—by proposing TokenWall, a runtime defense framework that monitors natural-language token flows (memory updates, tool arguments, inter-component communications) to intercept unsafe behavior before execution. The work matters because traditional LLM safety approaches assume single-turn interactions, but persistent agents create larger attack surfaces where unsafe content can propagate through reusable skills and stateful components.

title: Towards Mechanistically Understanding Why Memorized Knowledge Fails to Generalize in Large Language Model Finetuning
authors: Lu Dai, Ziyang Rao, Yili Wang, Hanqing Wang, Hao Liu et al.
summary: This paper identifies and mechanistically explains why large language models can memorize new facts during fine-tuning but fail to use those facts for reasoning — a problem called the "Knowing–Using Gap." The authors introduce a diagnostic technique called self-patching to trace where memorized knowledge gets stuck inside the model, then propose a simple fix that recovers most of the lost generalization performance.

title: Multi-Agent Firewall Architecture for Privacy Protection of Sensitive Data in Interactions with Language Models
authors: Hugo García Cuesta, Pablo Mateo Torrejón, Alfonso Sánchez-Macián
summary: This paper presents a privacy-protecting firewall system that sits between users and language models, intercepting and analyzing all interactions to prevent sensitive data leakage. The system combines rule-based detection with AI-driven semantic analysis across browser and programmatic interfaces, achieving 94.93% accuracy at blocking risky data sharing—solving a critical gap where LLM integration into workflows creates uncontrolled data exposure risks.

title: ASMR: Agentic Schema Generation for Ship Maintenance Report Writing
authors: Sohrab Namazi Nia, Amogh Dalal, Ning Sa, Peter Ly, Marti Zentmaier et al.
summary: This paper presents ASMR, a system that automatically discovers data schemas (structured templates) from historical ship maintenance reports using two specialized AI agents working together. Rather than manually defining what information should go in each report type, the system learns from past reports to generate compact, consistent schemas that help future report writers be more complete and clear — addressing a real problem in maritime operations where reports are critical but often inconsistent.

title: Reinforcing the Generation Order of Multimodal Masked Diffusion Models
authors: Yidong Ouyang, Zhe Wang, Sourav Bhabesh, Dmitriy Bespalov
summary: This paper improves diffusion models for multimodal tasks (text-to-image and image understanding) by learning the optimal order in which to generate tokens, rather than using a fixed sequence. The key insight is that a learnable control module trained with reinforcement learning can determine better generation orderings than model confidence alone, achieving ~4% improvements on text-to-image alignment and multimodal reasoning benchmarks.

title: Who Broke the System? Failure Localization in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Yufei Xia, Anjun Gao, Yueyang Quan, Zhuqing Liu, Minghong Fang
summary: This paper addresses a critical debugging challenge in LLM-based multi-agent systems: when a coordinated task fails, determining which agent caused the failure and at which step the trajectory became irreversibly misdirected. The authors propose AgentLocate, a framework that combines LLM-based judgment with multi-perspective verification to pinpoint failures with high accuracy, then uses this feedback to improve the diagnosis system itself. This matters because as LLM agents tackle complex problems through distributed coordination, effective failure diagnosis becomes essential for reliability and debugging.

title: Expressivity and Statistical Trade-offs in Diffusion Policy Learning
authors: Viet Vu, Renyuan Xu, Jiacheng Zhang, Yufei Zhang
summary: This paper analyzes diffusion-based policies—a method for learning action distributions in reinforcement learning by modeling them as solutions to diffusion processes. The key finding is that a single parameter (the drift Lipschitz constant K) governs a fundamental trade-off: larger K makes the policy more expressive (better approximation of optimal actions), but requires more data to learn reliably. The paper provides theoretical bounds and practical guidance on balancing this trade-off for finite-sample learning.

title: From Triggers to Emotions: A CPM-Grounded Appraisal Multi-Agent for Dynamic Emotional Evolution in Persona-Based Dialogue
authors: Jingyao Cai, Shuaijun Liu, Abdul Rehman, Yutong Guo, Qin Tian et al.
summary: This paper proposes CPM-MultiAgent, a framework that models how dialogue agents' emotions evolve dynamically during conversations, rather than treating emotions as fixed personality traits. It applies the Component Process Model (CPM)—a psychology theory where emotions emerge from appraising events—to create persona-based dialogue agents that respond emotionally to conversational triggers in healthcare, counseling, and storytelling applications.

title: A law of robustness for two-layer neural networks with arbitrary weights
authors: Yitzchak Shmalo
summary: This paper proves a fundamental law about neural network robustness: any two-layer neural network that memorizes noisy data must have a large Lipschitz constant (a measure of how sensitive the network is to input changes). The result holds for networks with arbitrary weight magnitudes, closing a gap left by prior work that required bounded weights. This matters because it reveals a deep trade-off between fitting data and maintaining stability — you cannot do both simultaneously without penalty.

title: Accurate, Interdisciplinary and Transparent Structure-property Understanding with Deep Native Structural Reasoning
authors: Chen Tang, Yizhou Wang, Jianyu Wu, Lintao Wang, Shixiang Tang et al.
summary: This paper introduces SciReasoner, a foundation model that predicts molecular and material properties by reasoning over structural information while explaining *why* predictions are made using scientific principles. Unlike black-box models, SciReasoner treats atomic coordinates, chemical bonds, and crystal symmetries as interpretable "evidence tokens" — the model shows which structural features drive predictions and whether they satisfy domain constraints. It outperforms prior methods across protein function prediction, chemical synthesis planning, and materials discovery, while generating explanations validated as scientifically sound by expert review.

title: The Key to Going Linear: Analysis-Driven Transformer Linearization
authors: Anna Kuzina, Paul N. Whatmough, Babak Ehteshami Bejnordi
summary: This paper addresses a fundamental bottleneck in transformer inference: the quadratic computational cost of self-attention when processing long contexts. The authors analyze what makes attention mechanisms work, then systematically redesign their components to achieve linear-time inference while preserving model quality. They demonstrate their approach scales to 32-billion-parameter models and matches the performance of more complex adaptive caching systems.

title: From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization
authors: Ying Chang, Jiahang Xu, Xuan Feng, Chenyuan Yang, Peng Cheng et al.
summary: This paper addresses a core challenge in agent optimization: when LLMs reflect on agent failures to improve policies, the execution traces they analyze are noisy, redundant, and contain irrelevant steps that distract from true root causes. The authors propose STRACE, a framework that filters traces at the batch level (removing redundant failures) and within each trace (identifying causally important steps via dependency analysis), enabling more precise optimization signals. On formal verification tasks, STRACE achieves a 1.4× improvement in success rate.

title: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass
authors: Victor Giannakouris, Immanuel Trummer
summary: This paper presents Jailbreak, a system that uses large language models to automatically generate code for reading database storage files directly, bypassing traditional database drivers like JDBC and ODBC. By having LLMs analyze database source code and documentation, the system synthesizes custom columnar data readers that produce Apache Arrow buffers consumable by modern analytics engines, achieving up to 27x speedups on analytical workloads while maintaining correctness.

title: Institutional Red-Teaming: Deployment Rules, Not Just Models, Causally Shape Multi-Agent AI Safety
authors: Yujiao Chen
summary: This paper introduces institutional red-teaming, a methodology for testing how deployment rules (not just model weights) causally shape the safety of multi-agent AI systems. The authors built IABench-CA, a benchmark with nearly 34,000 games, and discovered that simple rule changes can shift fatality rates by 22–58 percentage points—and that identity-targeting rules (which single out specific agents) consistently fail, even when they're theoretically fair, because language models learn to exploit the targeting signal. This matters because we typically evaluate AI safety by looking at individual models in isolation; this work shows that institutional structures around multi-agent AI are equally critical.

title: Selective Timestep Weighting and Advantage-Based Replay for Sample-Efficient Diffusion RLHF
authors: Eric Zhu, Abhinav Shrivastava, Soumik Mukhopadhyay
summary: This paper addresses the feedback inefficiency problem in applying reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to diffusion models — generative models that iteratively denoise random noise into coherent outputs. The authors propose two techniques: selective timestep weighting (emphasizing informative denoising steps) and advantage-based replay (reusing trajectories with high learning value). Together, these achieve up to 6× better sample efficiency without sacrificing generalization to new prompts.

title: Agon: Competitive Cross-Model RL with Implicit Rival Grading of Reasoning
authors: Vladislav Beliaev
summary: This paper introduces Agon, a competitive training method where two language models grade each other's reasoning on difficult problems. Instead of relying on a single external reward signal (like checking only final answers), the models compete by alternately solving problems and critiquing each other's work — forcing both to develop genuine reasoning skills rather than just longer outputs. On hard math problems, this approach roughly doubles the performance of standard RL methods.

title: Agent Delivery Engineering Predictive Reliability Framework
authors: Dexing Liu
summary: This paper addresses a critical blind spot in production LLM multi-agent systems: infrastructure monitoring can miss reliability degradation that isn't visible in standard metrics. The authors propose ADE-PRF, a framework that predicts system health 8 hours ahead by aggregating 20 disparate signals into a single "Trust Margin" metric, validated on 380,000+ real predictions across six agent types. The work matters because it enables teams to catch failing agents before they cause costly errors—a previously unsolved problem in deployed multi-agent systems.

title: ECGLight: Compute-Light Framework For Paper ECG Digitization and Myocardial Infarction Screening
authors: Shreyasvi Natraj, Cyrus Achtari, Felice Gragnano, Andrea Milzi, Marco Valgimigli et al.
summary: This paper presents ECGLight, a lightweight software pipeline that converts smartphone photos of paper ECGs into digital signals and screens for myocardial infarction (heart attack) in under 30 seconds on CPU-only devices. The system addresses a critical gap: millions of paper ECGs in remote clinics worldwide cannot access modern AI diagnostics due to poor connectivity and limited computing power, causing dangerous delays in detecting life-threatening heart conditions.

title: Any-Dimensional Learning by Sampling
authors: Eitan Levin, Venkat Chandrasekaran
summary: This paper develops a unified theoretical framework for understanding how machine learning models generalize when trained on small inputs but evaluated on larger ones—a problem arising in sequences, graphs, and point clouds of varying sizes. The authors use random sampling maps to characterize when and how well models can scale from small to large inputs, and provide explicit generalization and sketching rates for important function classes like graph neural networks and transformers.

title: How Data Shapes RoPE Frequency Usage: From Positional Scale Matching to Length Generalization
authors: Xinyi Wu, Siyuan Liu, Ali Jadbabaie
summary: This paper explains why transformer models using Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) prefer certain frequencies over others—a phenomenon that has been observed but not well understood. The authors show that frequency selection is driven by the structure of training data: the model learns to use frequencies that match the "width" of dependency relationships in that data. This insight connects to a practical problem: why do transformers struggle with longer contexts than they saw during training, and how can frequency scaling help them generalize better.

title: MedPMC: A Systematic Framework for Scaling High-Fidelity Medical Multimodal Data for Foundation Models
authors: Hyunjae Kim, Dain Kim, Pan Xiao, Serina S. Applebaum, Younjoon Chung et al.
summary: This paper introduces MedPMC, a framework that automatically extracts and aligns high-quality image-text pairs from 6.1 million PubMed Central articles to create a large-scale medical multimodal dataset. The resulting 11 million curated pairs significantly improve foundation models for medical imaging tasks — a CLIP-style model trained on MedPMC data outperforms existing biomedical baselines by 7.1 percentage points on zero-shot benchmarks and shows real-world gains in clinical settings like dermatology image retrieval.

title: Does Bielik Know What It Doesn't Know? Activation Dispersion Separates Entity Familiarity from Factual Reliability Across Model Scale
authors: Grzegorz Brzezinka
summary: This paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) internally "know" when they're about to hallucinate, by examining neural activation patterns before generating answers about entities. Using Polish language models (Bielik, 1.5B–11B parameters), the authors discover that activation dispersion measures can detect whether a model has seen an entity before with near-perfect accuracy—but this internal awareness does NOT predict whether the model will actually answer correctly. The key finding: models are aware of entity familiarity but rarely abstain, revealing a gap between what models internally know and what they output.

title: DiaLLM: An Investigation into the Robustness-Generation Gap in English Dialect Adaptation
authors: Jordan Painter, Dipankar Srirag, Adarsh Kappiyath, Diptesh Kanojia, Aditya Joshi et al.
summary: This paper investigates why large language models understand different English dialects (Australian, Indian, Northern British) but refuse to generate them, producing only standard US English instead. The authors systematically test various training methods—continual pretraining, fine-tuning, and alignment strategies—and find a surprising mismatch: benchmark improvements don't predict whether models actually produce recognizable dialect, and the training methods that optimize hardest for dialect don't match human preferences.

title: A hierarchical memory architecture overcomes context limits in long-horizon multi-agent computational modeling
authors: Shivendra G. Tewari, Holly Kimko
summary: This paper presents Ensemble QSP, a multi-agent system that enables large language models to work autonomously on long-horizon scientific tasks without running out of context. The key innovation is a three-layer hierarchical memory architecture that keeps the active context window bounded (median 301 tokens) by selectively retaining relevant information and discarding completed work—allowing the system to run indefinitely while maintaining reasoning quality. The authors demonstrate the approach on pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling, showing that it produces consistent, high-quality results across multiple LLM backends.

title: RL Post-Training Builds Compositional Reasoning Strategies
authors: Azwar Abdulsalam, Nishil Patel, Andrew Saxe
summary: This paper investigates whether reinforcement learning post-training can teach language models to discover genuinely new strategies by composing simpler skills, rather than just amplifying existing abilities. Using a controlled environment where all model outputs can be inspected, the authors show that RL discovers and reuses complex multi-step procedures that the base model rarely finds, while rejection fine-tuning (a simpler alternative) produces mostly invalid shortcuts.

title: Future Confidence Distillation in Large Language Models
authors: Sahil Kale
summary: This paper investigates how LLMs develop confidence about their answers over time, rather than treating confidence as a fixed property of final outputs. The authors show that confidence estimates made *after* generating an answer are much better calibrated than pre-generation estimates, and they propose "future confidence distillation"—a technique that learns to predict post-generation confidence using only the hidden representations available before generation begins, avoiding the computational cost of full answer generation.

title: A Unified Detection Framework for AI-Related Content and Artifacts
authors: Xifeng Zhang, Tao Hu, Yijie Peng, Wan Tian
summary: This paper presents a unified detection framework for identifying AI-generated content and artifacts—including LLM-generated text, hallucinations, watermarks, and adversarial examples—using Mahalanobis distance scores (MDS) computed on deep neural network representations. The work matters because as AI systems proliferate, robust detection of synthetic or manipulated content becomes critical for oversight, safety, and regulatory compliance across multiple domains.

title: Fast Rates for Semi-Supervised Learning via Data-Augmentation Graph Regularization
authors: Adam M. Oberman
summary: This paper explains why semi-supervised learning with data augmentation works so well in practice—achieving supervised-level accuracy with far fewer labeled examples. The authors prove that data augmentation creates a similarity graph on unlabeled data, and learning on this graph achieves a fast $O(1/n_L)$ convergence rate (compared to the standard $O(1/\sqrt{n_L})$ supervised rate). This is the first theoretical justification for the empirical success of augmentation-based semi-supervised learning without unrealistic assumptions.

title: Social-spatial dependencies for learning visual navigation
authors: Patrick Govoni, Pawel Romanczuk
summary: This paper studies how individual neural network agents learn to navigate in groups, showing that social information and spatial proximity trigger phase transitions in behavior — from independent navigation to following to collision avoidance. The work demonstrates that group dynamics and embodied interactions fundamentally shape what strategies organisms learn, challenging the assumption that individual behavior can be understood in isolation.

title: Multi-Agent Robotic Control with Onboard Vision-Language Models
authors: Kajetan Rachwał, Maciej Majek, Bartłomiej Boczek, Jakub Matejczyk, Dominik Matejkowski et al.
summary: This paper demonstrates a multi-agent robotic system that runs entirely on onboard hardware (a modest AMD mini PC) to control a mobile manipulator in warehouse tasks. Rather than relying on cloud compute or large foundation models, the system uses compact vision-language models (3–20B parameters) coordinated by specialized agents, including a novel "Megamind" orchestrator that solves long-horizon planning problems. The work shows that practical, autonomous robotic control is achievable with distributed onboard agents, not expensive external compute.

title: A Large Language Model-Driven Agent-Based Modeling Framework with Multi-Round Communication for Simulating Vaccine Opinion Dynamics
authors: Bo Zhang, Na Jiang
summary: This paper embeds a large language model (Qwen3-8B) into agent-based simulations to model how people form and change opinions about vaccines. The key insight is that different cognitive modules — like memory and prompt diversity — have measurably different effects on emergent group-level opinion patterns, and the framework successfully reproduces known non-linear social influence effects seen in real data.

title: Dynamic neural manifolds for flexible closed-loop control on neuromorphic hardware
authors: Oskar von Seeler, Christian Tetzlaff, Andrew Lehr
summary: This paper demonstrates how spiking neural networks (brain-inspired neural circuits that communicate via discrete spike events) can implement flexible control behaviors by organizing neural activity along dynamic, low-dimensional manifolds — geometric structures that compress high-dimensional neural activity into simpler patterns. The authors implement this on neuromorphic hardware (the SpiNNaker 2 chip), showing a robot can use sensory feedback to rapidly switch between different behaviors and navigate a maze, bridging neuroscience insights with real-time robotic control.

title: Multi-Agent AI Control: Distributed Attacks Hamper Per-Instance Monitors
authors: Oliver Makins, Orazio Angelini, Zohreh Shams, Mary Phuong
summary: This paper studies how AI control techniques — methods designed to prevent a single AI agent from acting against its operator's goals — fail when multiple agents coordinate to attack shared infrastructure. The authors build FakeLab, a synthetic lab environment with 86 benign tasks and 4 attack scenarios, and show that per-agent monitoring becomes progressively less effective as more agents work together on the same malicious objective. This matters because real AI deployments run many models simultaneously, and current safety monitoring assumes single-agent threat models.

title: From Atomic Actions to Standard Operating Procedures: Iterative Tool Optimization for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
authors: Haipeng Ding, Yuexiang Xie, Zhewei Wei, Yaliang Li, Bolin Ding
summary: This paper addresses a fundamental inefficiency in LLM agents: they typically work with low-level atomic tools (like "read file" or "search query"), forcing them to repeatedly reconstruct complex multi-step workflows from scratch. The authors propose EvoSOP, a framework that lets agents automatically synthesize these atomic actions into reusable Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)—higher-level tools that bundle multi-step logic. The result is agents that learn faster, succeed more often, and require fewer interaction steps to complete tasks.

title: Operational Reframing and Approval-Framed Delegation in Multi-Agent LLM Safety
authors: Lifei Liu, Haoran Yu, Xiaochong Jiang, Su Wang, Pin Qian et al.
summary: This paper dissects how multi-agent LLM systems (where one model plans and another executes) behave differently from single-model systems when faced with harmful requests. Rather than measuring a single "safety difference," the authors identify three separate mechanisms at play: harmful requests get reframed as legitimate work, planners may refuse requests, and executors may comply more readily when told a planner already approved the task. They show these mechanisms vary dramatically by model and scenario, meaning you can't predict deployed safety just by testing a single model in isolation.

title: Progressive Crystallization: Turning Agent Exploration into Deterministic, Lower-Cost Workflows in Production
authors: Arun Malik
summary: This paper solves a critical problem in AI operations: AI agents that handle IT tasks are expensive because every execution requires full language model inference, even when solving the same problem repeatedly. The authors introduce "progressive crystallization," a system that learns from agent behavior over time and automatically converts successful patterns into cheap, deterministic workflows—like turning a thoughtful AI decision into a simple if-then rule. In production testing across tens of thousands of cloud incidents, the approach cut per-incident costs by 70% while tripling the fraction of fully automated (non-LLM-requiring) executions.

title: Best-Arm Identification with Generative Proxy
authors: Tianyi Ma, Hanzhang Qin, Ruihao Zhu, Jierui Zuo
summary: This paper studies how to efficiently identify the best option among many alternatives when getting true rewards is expensive, but cheap proxy predictions (like LLM outputs) are available. The key insight is that by learning the correlation between proxy and reward online, a standard control-variate adjustment lets you reduce the number of costly samples needed—but you must carefully estimate residual variance without breaking the statistical guarantees. The authors propose PROBE, an algorithm that maintains valid confidence bounds and achieves near-oracle sample complexity in experiments ranging from synthetic problems to real auto-loan pricing.

title: LLM-powered reasoning in agent-based modeling
authors: Sifat Afroj Moon, Dakotah Maguire, Adam Spannaus, Joe Tuccillo, Maksudul Alam et al.
summary: This paper combines agent-based modeling (ABM) — a simulation technique that models thousands or millions of individuals and their interactions — with large language models (LLMs) to make those simulations more adaptive and realistic. Instead of relying on fixed rules for how people behave, the researchers use LLMs to predict human decision-making in real time. They demonstrate the approach on COVID-19 spread in Salt Lake County, showing that LLMs can help ABMs respond dynamically to changing conditions and inform policy decisions.

title: Heat-Kernel Entropy Profiles and Geometric Effective Sample Size for Weighted Measures on Manifolds
authors: Kisung You
summary: This paper introduces heat-kernel entropy profiles, a new way to measure the quality and geometry-awareness of weighted particle samples on curved spaces (manifolds). Unlike existing methods that only count effective particles, this approach understands the underlying geometry—it detects when particles cluster, duplicate, or spread unevenly across the curved surface. The work matters because weighted samples appear everywhere in machine learning: importance sampling, neural network approximations, and Bayesian inference all rely on good particle representations.

title: ELSA3D: Elastic Semantic Anchoring for Unified 3D Understanding and Generation
authors: Tianjiao Yu, Xinzhuo Li, Yifan Shen, Onkar Susladkar, Yuanzhe Liu et al.
summary: ELSA3D is a unified 3D foundation model that bridges language and 3D geometry through a novel architecture called elastic semantic anchoring. Unlike existing methods that flatten text and 3D data into a single sequence, ELSA3D intelligently routes semantic information across different geometric scales, achieving better performance on image-to-3D, text-to-3D generation, and 3D captioning while cutting computational cost roughly in half.

title: Graph Convolutional Attention: A Spectral Perspective on Graph Denoising and Diffusion
authors: Shervin Khalafi, Igor Krawczuk, Sergio Rozada, Charilaos Kanatsoulis, Antonio G Marques et al.
summary: This paper explains why standard attention mechanisms underperform for denoising graphs and proposes Graph Convolutional Attention (GCA) as a fix. The key insight is that linear attention learns only a single denoising filter averaged across the training distribution, but real graphs vary spectrally (their frequency components differ), requiring adaptive filters. GCA outperforms linear attention by using the input graph's spectrum directly, with provable guarantees. The work matters because graph denoising is core to generative models like diffusion-based graph generation, and better understanding attention mechanisms helps build more principled architectures.

title: Rethinking Indic AI from a Lens of Cultural Heritage Preservation
authors: Aparna Madva, Sharath Srivatsa, Srinath Srinivasa, Tulika Saha
summary: This paper examines how AI development in India risks homogenizing linguistic and cultural diversity while offering potential for inclusion and access. The authors survey the evolution of Natural Language Processing (NLP) for Indian languages, analyze the structural challenges these languages pose (morphological complexity, multiple scripts, dialects), and propose "Culture Sensing"—a new research direction using hermeneutic reasoning to build AI that is both equitable across low-resource languages and culturally meaningful.

title: Hierarchical Acoustic-Semantic Modeling: Modality Separation and Semantic Coherence for Full-Duplex SLMs
authors: Zhenyu Liu, Yunxin Li, Xuanyu Zhang, Qixun Teng, Shenyuan Jiang et al.
summary: This paper tackles a fundamental problem in full-duplex spoken language models (SLMs) — systems that must listen and respond simultaneously — by diagnosing why acoustic (speech) and semantic (meaning) processing interfere with each other during training. The authors show that this interference stems from conflicting gradients when both modalities share the same neural network layers, and propose Lychee-FD, a framework that separates these modalities while keeping them coordinated through a dedicated alignment channel. The result is more natural, intelligent conversational speech systems that outperform prior work by substantial margins.

title: The Large Cancer Assistant (LCA): A Model-Agnostic Orchestration Framework for Scalable Clinical Decision Support in Oncology
authors: Ghassen Marrakchi, Basarab Matei
summary: This paper introduces the Large Cancer Assistant (LCA), a framework that orchestrates multiple AI models for clinical oncology decision support without being tightly coupled to any specific model. The key innovation is architectural: by decoupling data intake, routing logic, and AI inference, the system can swap out models, handle incomplete data gracefully, and integrate with hospital IT systems more flexibly than traditional monolithic approaches.

title: RSF-GLLM: Bridging the Semantic Gap in Multi-Hop Knowledge Graph QA via Recurrent Soft-Flow and Decoupled LLM Generation
authors: Sambaran Bandyopadhyay, Ananth Muppidi
summary: This paper tackles multi-hop question answering over knowledge graphs—finding answers by reasoning across multiple connected facts rather than single lookup. The core problem: traditional pipelines that retrieve facts then read answers break the ability for the retriever to learn, especially when intermediate facts don't share words with the question. RSF-GLLM solves this by decoupling learnable graph reasoning (via soft continuous scores) from answer generation (via fine-tuned LLM), achieving competitive accuracy with lower computational cost.

title: Data Analysis in the Wild: Benchmarking Large Language Models Against Real-World Data Complexities
authors: So Hasegawa, Shailaja Keyur Sampat, Lei Liu, Wei-Peng Chen
summary: This paper introduces DataGovBench, a benchmark that tests how well large language models (LLMs) perform on real-world data analysis tasks using government open datasets. The benchmark covers two challenges: answering complex questions about multi-table datasets and discovering expert-level insights through exploratory analysis. The work matters because existing benchmarks focus on simple fact retrieval from small tables and miss the messy reality of data analytics — the paper shows that state-of-the-art LLMs still fall short on these practical tasks.

title: WordVoice: Explicit and Decoupled Multi-Dimensional Word-Level Control for LLM-Based TTS
authors: Sihang Nie, Jinxin Ji, Xiaofen Xing, Deyi Tuo, Chengbin Jin et al.
summary: WordVoice is a framework that gives precise, word-level control over speech synthesis using large language models. The key innovation is transforming implicit (black-box) LLM-based text-to-speech into explicit, controllable generation by introducing acoustic planning at the token level and fine-grained modulation at the waveform level. This matters because existing systems lack the granular control needed for professional applications like audiobook narration and video dubbing, where you need to precisely control when words start/end, their energy, pitch, and tone.

title: A Hardware-Aware Open-Source Framework for Design Space Exploration of Mixed-Signal Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Sayma Nowshin Chowdhury, Vineeta Nair, Taseen Forhad, Aishwarya Natarajan, Corey Hart et al.
summary: This paper presents an open-source simulation framework for designing energy-efficient neuromorphic chips — computers inspired by biological brains that use spiking neural networks (SNNs). The framework helps engineers explore trade-offs between accuracy, power consumption, and physical chip size by modeling how real hardware components behave, not just idealized software versions. This matters because neuromorphic chips promise dramatic energy savings for edge AI (on-device computing), but designing them requires tools that accurately predict real-world performance.

title: From Voting to Agent Collaboration: Answer-Type-Aware LLM Pipelines for BioASQ 14b
authors: Taeyun Roh, Eunha Lee, Wonjune Jang, Sohyun Chung, Junha Jung et al.
summary: This paper presents a question-type-aware LLM framework for biomedical question answering in the BioASQ challenge, where different answer formats (yes/no, factoid, list) receive tailored inference strategies rather than a one-size-fits-all approach. The work demonstrates that routing questions to specialized pipelines—including multi-agent collaboration for list questions—improves both answer accuracy and evidence grounding, achieving first place in the factoid subtask of BioASQ 14b Batch 4.

title: Danus: Orchestrating Mathematical Reasoning Agents with Fact-Graph Memory
authors: Jihao Liu, Guoxiong Gao, Zeming Sun, Bin Wu, Shurui Liu et al.
summary: This paper presents Danus, a system that orchestrates multiple AI agents to collaboratively solve research-level mathematics problems by sharing a unified "fact graph" — a structured memory of verified mathematical claims and their dependencies. Rather than having a single agent solve problems linearly, Danus coordinates parallel proof search across multiple worker agents while maintaining correctness through verification and organization. The work demonstrates that this approach can tackle genuine open problems in algebraic geometry and combinatorics, showing a practical path toward AI systems that can contribute to mathematical research.

title: TILDE: TILt-based Distributional Erasure for Concept Unlearning
authors: Naveen George, Naoki Murata, Yuhta Takida, Konda Reddy Mopuri, Yuki Mitsufuji
summary: This paper addresses concept unlearning in text-to-image diffusion models—the ability to remove unwanted concepts (copyrighted styles, trademarked characters, etc.) after training while maintaining the model's quality on other tasks. The key insight is framing unlearning as a distributional alignment problem rather than just erasing a concept: the goal is to match the distribution of a reference model trained from scratch without the unwanted data, not just suppress target images. The paper proposes TILDE, which uses energy-tilted distributions and gradient flow learning to achieve both strong forgetting and strong retention.

title: What Images Cannot Say: Language-Guided Olfactory Representation Learning
authors: Eleftherios Tsonis, Xi Wang, Vicky Kalogeiton
summary: This paper introduces SCENT, a framework that learns to represent odors (from electronic-nose sensors) by using language as a bridge between visual images and smell signals. The key insight is that many smells come from contextual environmental factors invisible in pixels — so the researchers use Vision-Language Models to describe what a scene looks like and what it might *smell* like, then train an olfactory encoder to align smell data with these multimodal descriptions. The work matters because it tackles a genuinely hard problem: making sensory systems (smell) learnable from visual data, which requires semantic reasoning beyond raw pixel values.

title: Learning to Throw Objects Safely in Multi-Obstacle Environments
authors: Mohammadreza Kasaei, Klemen Voncina, Hamidreza Kasaei
summary: This paper addresses robotic throwing in cluttered environments—a challenging task that requires a robot to toss objects into a target basket while navigating around randomly placed obstacles. The authors introduce a potential field representation that encodes both target attraction and obstacle repulsion on a compact grid, enabling reinforcement learning policies to generalize across different obstacle configurations. Real-robot experiments achieve up to 90% success in cluttered scenes, demonstrating practical sim-to-real transfer.

title: A Function-Space Dichotomy for Compositional Learning: Exponential Sub-Optimality of the Neural Tangent Kernel
authors: Arkaprabha Ganguli, Emil Constantinescu
summary: This paper explains a long-observed mystery: why trained neural networks generalize better than their neural tangent kernel (NTK) limit on compositional tasks. Using functions on the unit circle, the authors prove that NTK regression can require exponentially more samples than necessary—the gap is a fundamental mismatch between the kernel's smoothness assumptions and compositional structure in the target function, not a quirk of training dynamics.

title: Training-Free Acceleration for Vision-Language-Action Models with Action Caching and Refinement
authors: Ryuji Oi, Hikari Otsuka, Kosuke Matsushima, Yuki Ichikawa, Masato Motomura et al.
summary: This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models — the slow iterative denoising process needed to generate robot actions. The authors propose ActionCache, a plug-and-play cache that reuses past intermediate action computations to speed up inference by up to 34×, while maintaining task success rates. This matters because real-time robotic control requires fast inference, and existing VLA models (particularly flow-matching variants) are too slow for deployment.

title: Quantitative Gaussian-Process limits of Tensor Programs
authors: Andrea Agazzi, Eloy Mosig García, Dario Trevisan
summary: This paper proves quantitative bounds on how closely finite neural networks approximate their infinite-width Gaussian-process limits using tensor programs. The authors show the approximation error shrinks at a rate of roughly 1/√width—meaning doubling network width cuts the error by ~30%—and their framework applies to feed-forward, recurrent, and transformer architectures without requiring architecture-specific proofs.

title: Arbitrage-Free Multi-Maturity Risk-Neutral Marginals
authors: Hao Qin, Ruozhong Yang, Charlie Che, Liming Feng
summary: This paper solves a practical problem in quantitative finance: converting market option prices into risk-neutral probability distributions that are mathematically consistent (arbitrage-free) and computationally tractable. The authors propose an explicit construction method that works directly from discrete option prices, guarantees no arbitrage opportunities, and produces distributions with closed-form solutions for density, quantiles, and sampling — enabling downstream applications like scenario analysis and tail-risk measurement without compromise.

title: Can Reinforcement Learning Efficiently Discover Price Manipulation?
authors: Ioanna-Yvonni Tsaknaki, Andrea Macrì, Fabrizio Lillo
summary: This paper investigates whether a reinforcement learning (RL) agent can discover price manipulation strategies more effectively than traditional model-based approaches in financial markets. The researchers compare a model-free RL method (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) against a model-based optimizer in a controlled market microstructure model, finding that RL can discover profitable manipulative strategies even with limited data and despite the model-based approach having correct specification—but also revealing serious risks of deploying such algorithms without safeguards.

title: Information Limits and Attractor Dynamics in Economies of Frontier LLM Agents: A Pre-Registered Test
authors: Cheng Qian
summary: This paper tests whether frontier LLM agents (Claude Opus 4.8) in simulated economies behave according to information-theoretic predictions about wealth growth and population coordination. Using pre-registered experiments with frozen predictions and cached model outputs, the authors confirm that relative wealth growth tracks relative information in parimutuel-coupled markets, but find that LLM populations fail to exhibit the smooth noise-stabilized diversity that mean-field theory predicts — instead showing sharp phase transitions and bistability near control boundaries.

title: MCP-Enabled Agentic AI for Autonomous IPoDWDM Network Lifecycle Automation
authors: Chunmin Xia, Jakub Harbaczewski, Nikhil Dsilva, Julie Raulin, Dominic Schneider et al.
summary: This paper demonstrates an agentic AI architecture that automates the complete lifecycle management of IPoDWDM networks (IP over Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing — technology that carries data across fiber optic cables). The system uses Model Context Protocol (MCP) to enable AI agents to control network operations in a vendor-independent way, with live validation on real hardware showing closed-loop automation from planning through execution.

title: Delay-Aware Active Triangulation with Uncertainty-Driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Counter-UAS
authors: Seungwook Lee, David Hyunchul Shim
summary: This paper tackles 3D localization of aerial targets (drones) using multiple coordinated camera-equipped agents that must communicate and act despite communication delays. The key innovation is a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly accounts for latency (using Age-of-Information tracking) and uncertainty sources, achieving 0.547 m localization accuracy in simulation. This matters because real-world Counter-UAS systems must operate in noisy, delayed environments—ignoring these factors degrades performance dramatically.

title: No Subspace to Track: Non-Identifiability and Optimizer State in Low-Rank Training
authors: Noel Thomas
summary: This paper reveals that low-rank subspace tracking—the core assumption behind memory-efficient optimizers like GaLore—is fundamentally unreliable due to estimator noise. The authors show that subspace estimates from different minibatches disagree as much as estimates separated by many training steps, meaning there is no stable "object" to track. Instead, they propose treating subspace refreshes as coordinate changes for optimizer state, leading to LDAdam, which outperforms patched GaLore while clarifying why and when low-rank training actually works.

title: StateFuse: Deterministic Conflict-Preserving Memory for Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Sergey Volkov, Yang Li, Ye Luo
summary: StateFuse is a memory system for multi-agent applications that preserves conflicting information instead of hiding it behind overwrite rules. Rather than collapsing disagreements, StateFuse explicitly tracks contradictions and provides tools to surface, audit, and correct them — making multi-agent systems safer and more transparent when agents disagree.

title: Width-Robust Learnability in Mean-Field Bayesian Neural Networks
authors: Dmitry Vaintrob, Kaarel Hänni
summary: This paper proves that Bayesian neural networks at infinite width have the same learning complexity as large finite networks—they don't gain spurious power from their infinite capacity. The key insight is that at the "mean-field" scaling regime (where neural networks learn features rather than just adjusting output weights), learning a target function requires polynomial reduced entropy (a measure of prior cost) if and only if it's learnable from polynomial samples. This matters because it explains why infinite-width limits are theoretically justified: they capture the actual inductive bias of practical networks.

title: LLM-Driven Evolutionary Generation of Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization Algorithms
authors: Georgios Laskaris, Reuben Brasher, Niki van Stein, Elena Raponi, Thomas Bäck et al.
summary: This paper uses large language models as creative operators within evolutionary algorithms to automatically design new multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) algorithms. Rather than manually engineering algorithm components, the authors let an LLM generate and mutate algorithm code across hundreds of evolutionary iterations, then benchmark the results on standard optimization problems. The best generated algorithms match or beat state-of-the-art baselines while running 60× faster on synthetic problems and 3.4× faster on real engineering tasks.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Battery Management of Autonomous Order Pickers
authors: Taniya Shaji, Abhay Sobhanan, Christof Defryn
summary: This paper applies deep reinforcement learning to optimize battery charging decisions for autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) in warehouse operations. The problem is inherently dynamic—robots must decide when and where to charge as orders arrive unpredictably—and involves coordination among multiple agents, making traditional fixed scheduling rules suboptimal. The proposed PPO-based approach learns both charging station selection and duration, achieving 6% higher order-completion rates and reduced total charging time compared to existing methods.

title: To Retain or to Adapt? Generalizing Continual Learning
authors: Giulia Lanzillotta, Mandana Samiei, Doina Precup, Razvan Pascanu, Claire Vernade
summary: This paper challenges a foundational assumption in continual learning: that lifelong learners should retain all previous knowledge to match a Joint-Task Learning (JTL) solution. Instead, the authors argue that in changing environments, prioritizing retention can actually hurt real-time performance. They formalize this tradeoff through a new metric (Average Lifelong Error) and prove that historical knowledge becomes a liability once task duration exceeds a critical threshold. The work matters because it reframes a 20-year-old problem—most continual learning papers optimize for preventing forgetting—and shows this goal may be misaligned with practical performance.

title: Deep Neural Variation Spaces: A Unifying Perspective on Depth and Complexity
authors: Julia Nakhleh, Robert D. Nowak
summary: This paper develops a unified mathematical framework for understanding deep neural networks by studying them as function spaces with controlled complexity. Rather than analyzing networks through parameter counts or specific activation types, the authors define recursive ℓ¹-bounded function spaces that work across many activation functions and depths, then prove that this notion of complexity actually limits what networks can represent—counterintuitively showing that depth may offer less advantage than commonly believed when complexity is properly controlled.

title: SHARC: SHAP-Based Interpretability in Machine Learning Risk Models for Regulatory Capital under ICAAP and CCAR
authors: Ujjwala Vadrevu
summary: This paper solves a critical regulatory problem: machine learning models like Gaussian Process Regression predict financial risk better than traditional methods, but regulators can't audit them because they're "black boxes." The authors apply SHAP (a game-theory-based explanation technique) to make these models transparent, decomposing stress-test capital requirements into interpretable components that regulators can trace and validate.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization via Direct On-Policy Distillation
authors: Shiyuan Feng, Huan-ang Gao, Haohan Chi, Hanlin Wu, Zhilong Zhang et al.
summary: This paper addresses a practical bottleneck in scaling language model training: running expensive reinforcement learning (RL) on every new, larger model is computationally wasteful. Instead, the authors propose running RL on a smaller, cheaper model, then transfer what it learned to improve a larger target model using a technique called Direct On-Policy Distillation (Direct-OPD). The key insight is to transfer not the weak model's final policy, but the *change* that RL induced—treating this as an implicit reward signal for the stronger model.

title: LLM-as-a-Verifier: A General-Purpose Verification Framework
authors: Jacky Kwok, Shulu Li, Pranav Atreya, Yuejiang Liu, Yixing Jiang et al.
summary: This paper introduces LLM-as-a-Verifier, a framework that uses language models to evaluate the correctness of solutions by computing continuous probability-based scores rather than discrete ratings. The key innovation is treating verification as a scalable axis for improving LLM capabilities—by increasing score granularity, repeating evaluations, and decomposing scoring criteria, the framework achieves state-of-the-art results on code generation, software engineering, robotics, and medical reasoning benchmarks. This matters because verification (checking if answers are right) is often easier and cheaper than generation (producing answers), making it a practical lever for improving agentic systems.

title: Search Beyond What Can Be Taught: Evolving the Knowledge Boundary in Agentic Visual Generation
authors: Haozhe Wang, Weijia Feng, Jinpeng Yu, Che Liu, Ping Nie et al.
summary: This paper identifies a critical failure mode in visual generation systems: they confidently generate incorrect content when asked about concepts outside their training data (new characters, recent events, niche entities). The authors propose SearchGen, an agentic framework that teaches generators when to use external search tools, and release a benchmark (SearchGen-Bench) with 20K prompts showing that frontier models score only 21–28/100 on out-of-knowledge requests. The work matters because it tackles the real-world problem of making visual generators useful for unbounded, evolving user requests rather than only what they've seen in training.

title: What Does a Discrete Diffusion Model Learn?
authors: Rodrigo Casado Noguerales, Bernhard Schölkopf, Thomas Hofmann, Aran Raoufi
summary: This paper provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for understanding what discrete diffusion models actually learn during training. The authors prove that regardless of how you parameterize the model (as a denoiser, score function, or bridge predictor), these are mathematically equivalent representations of the same underlying object — the reverse jump rate of a Markov chain. The key insight is that the optimal training objective for any discrete diffusion model has an irreducible cost equal to the entropy of the data, and this framework unifies several existing diffusion model variants (MDM, UDM, SEDD, GIDD) as special cases.

title: CompactionRL: Reinforcement Learning with Context Compaction for Long-Horizon Agents
authors: Yujiang Li, Zhenyu Hou, Yi Jing, Jie Tang, Yuxiao Dong
summary: This paper tackles a fundamental constraint in long-horizon AI agents: language models have finite context windows that can be exceeded during extended task execution. CompactionRL is a reinforcement learning method that trains LLM agents to both solve tasks and generate summaries of past interactions, allowing the model to "compress" its context and continue working on problems that would otherwise exceed memory limits. The method achieves significant improvements on challenging benchmarks like SWE-bench (software engineering tasks), showing that learned compaction outperforms naive truncation.

title: Cortex: A Bidirectionally Aligned Embodied Agent Framework for Long-horizon Manipulation
authors: Jiaqi Peng, Xiqian Yu, Delin Feng, Yuqiang Yang, Wenzhe Cai et al.
summary: Cortex is a framework that enables robots to complete long, multi-step manipulation tasks by combining a high-level vision-language model (VLM) for planning with a low-level vision-language-action (VLA) model for execution. The key innovation is a "bidirectional alignment" layer that translates abstract plans into concrete, executable skill primitives—solving a fundamental mismatch where planners think in semantic goals but robots execute physical motions.

title: Fitted Occupancy-Ratio Evaluation without Bellman Completeness
authors: Lars van der Laan, Nathan Kallus
summary: This paper proposes FORE (Fitted Occupancy-Ratio Evaluation), a new method for estimating occupancy ratios in offline reinforcement learning — a setting where you learn from fixed, collected data rather than active exploration. The key insight is that you can estimate these ratios using a simpler recursion (an "adjoint Bellman" equation) that only requires the occupancy ratio itself to be representable in your function class, avoiding stronger technical assumptions that prior methods needed. This matters because occupancy ratios are fundamental tools for correcting bias when evaluating policies on offline data.

title: GaP: A Graph-as-Policy Multi-Agent Self-Learning Harness For Variational Automation Tasks
authors: Kaiyuan Chen, Shuangyu Xie, Letian Fu, Justin Yu, William Pacini et al.
summary: This paper presents GaP (Graph-as-Policy), a system that uses AI agents to automatically design robotic task programs by representing them as computation graphs — networks of perception, planning, and control operations. The key insight is that rather than learning a black-box neural policy or hand-coding rigid procedures, GaP generates interpretable graph structures and iteratively refines them through simulation, achieving high reliability on real-world robotic tasks with geometric variation that typically defeat standard learning approaches.

title: REDDIT: Correcting Model-Generated Timestamp Drift in ASR without Forgetting via Replay-Based Distribution Editing
authors: Cheng-Kang Chou, Ming-To Chuang, Ke-Han Lu, Chan-Jan Hsu, Hung-yi Lee
summary: Modern automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems can generate timestamps alongside transcriptions, but this paper identifies a critical problem: timestamps drift during long non-speech segments (silence, pauses), making the time alignment unreliable even when the words themselves are correct. The authors propose REDDIT, a lightweight fine-tuning method that fixes timestamp accuracy without degrading the model's overall transcription quality—a challenge that naive retraining fails at.

title: Faithfulness to Refusal: A Causal Audit of Neuron Selectors
authors: Ananth Eswar, Pratinav Seth, Utsav Avaiya, Vinay Kumar Sankarapu
summary: This paper tests whether attribution methods (techniques that identify which neurons matter) actually identify *causally* important neurons in language models, rather than just statistically correlated ones. The authors show that popular attribution methods can fail to identify truly causal neurons, and they demonstrate this by testing whether neurons identified as important for "refusal" (rejecting harmful requests) actually install refusal behavior when edited—finding that many high-ranking neurons don't causally matter, and that refusal is implemented redundantly across different neuron sets.

title: Selective Disclosure Watermarking for Large Language Models
authors: Xuyang Chen, Xiang Li, Yangxinyu Xie, Qi Long
summary: This paper introduces HeRo (Hierarchical Vocabulary Routing), a watermarking method for LLM-generated text that lets different parties verify only the portions of an embedded message they're authorized to see. Unlike existing watermarking approaches that require full message exposure to verify any part, HeRo enables fine-grained access control over watermark disclosure—solving a real privacy problem in authentication and IP protection scenarios.

title: OptiAgent: End-to-End Optimization Modeling via Multi-Agent Iterative Refinement
authors: Adriana Laurindo Monteiro, Nayse Fagundes, Gabriel Mattos Langeloh, Gustavo de Oliveira Kanno, Priscila Louise Aguirre et al.
summary: OptiAgent is a multi-agent system that converts natural language descriptions of optimization problems into both mathematical formulations and executable solver code. The key innovation is a multi-loop validation architecture with four specialized feedback mechanisms that catch different types of errors (misinterpretation, structural defects, mathematical inconsistencies, validation failures, code errors), enabling iterative self-correction. This matters because optimization problem formulation is tedious and error-prone; automating it could make Operations Research accessible to non-experts and accelerate problem-solving workflows.

title: TREK: Distill to Explore, Reinforce to Refine
authors: Yuanda Xu, Zhengze Zhou, Kayhan Behdin, Jelena Markovic-Voronov, Hejian Sang et al.
summary: This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in reinforcement learning for language models: when a model lacks the internal capability to solve hard problems, standard policy optimization (like GRPO) gets stuck because it can't explore beyond what the model already knows. TREK solves this by first using distillation from a teacher to expand the model's solution repertoire, then refining those solutions with standard RL—achieving significant improvements on mathematical reasoning (AIME benchmarks) and agent tasks without requiring access to the teacher's internals.

title: PiSAs: Benchmarking Contextual Integrity in Multi-User Agentic Systems
authors: Shubham Gupta, Nazanin Mohammadi Sepahvand, Abhinav Kumar, Cem Subakan, Spandana Gella et al.
summary: This paper introduces PiSAs, a benchmark for measuring privacy leaks in multi-user LLM-based agent systems where multiple users share infrastructure. The core problem: as LLM agents move from personal assistants to organizational tools, sensitive information can leak not just in final outputs but also across inter-agent messages and shared memory. Existing privacy benchmarks miss this because they focus on single-user scenarios; PiSAs fills that gap by testing whether agents correctly identify what information is appropriate for each task and which users should access it.

title: How Much is Left? LLMs Linearly Encode Their Remaining Output Length
authors: Mohamed Amine Merzouk, Dmitri Carpov, Mirko Bronzi, Damiano Fornasiere, Adam Oberman
summary: This paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) internally estimate how many tokens remain in their output while generating. Using linear probes (simple machine-learning classifiers) on frozen model activations, the authors find that response length is predictable from the hidden states of frozen 7–8B parameter models, even before generation begins. This matters because it provides empirical evidence that LLMs maintain implicit "plans" about output structure — a window into how these models organize their reasoning and generation.

title: Curated retrieval versus open web search in public AI information services: a coverage-trust trade-off
authors: Hafsteinn Einarsson, Hafsteinn Birgir Einarsson, Jón Gunnar Ólafsson, Jón Gunnar Þorsteinsson
summary: This paper evaluates whether AI-powered public information services can reliably answer citizens' questions while citing trustworthy sources. Researchers tested two approaches—using a curated knowledge base versus open web search—by having domain experts rate 449 answers about the European Union on a quality rubric and flag problematic sources. They found that web search answers more questions but cited untrustworthy or irrelevant sources in over a third of cases, while curated sources were trustworthy but limited in scope; the work highlights that source trustworthiness is largely invisible in current AI systems despite being critical for public institutions.

title: An event-driven framework for fly-inspired visual motion detection
authors: Qinbing Fu, Jingyu Huang, Yan Xie, Jigen Peng, Yuchao Tang
summary: This paper combines event cameras (sensors that detect brightness changes asynchronously) with fly-inspired neural circuits to detect motion in video fast and efficiently. The approach is training-free, interpretable, and runs in real-time on embedded hardware — making it practical for autonomous systems that need quick, reliable motion detection in challenging lighting conditions.

title: Noisy-Channel Minimum Bayes Risk Decoding
authors: Yusuke Sakai, Hidetaka Kamigaito, Taro Watanabe
summary: This paper improves text generation quality by introducing a noisy-channel decomposition of Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding — a method that selects outputs by maximizing expected utility rather than just picking the single most likely output. The key insight is that evaluation metrics treat hypothesis-to-reference and reference-to-hypothesis comparisons asymmetrically, and the authors show how to properly account for both directions using a four-component probabilistic framework. This leads to more interpretable and potentially better decoding strategies.

title: Unified Audio Intelligence Without Regressing on Text Intelligence
authors: Zhifeng Kong, Sang-gil Lee, Jaehyeon Kim, Boxin Wang, Zihan Liu et al.
summary: This paper introduces Audex, a unified audio-text language model that processes both audio and text through a single Transformer decoder without sacrificing text-based reasoning or knowledge. The key innovation is showing that you can add strong audio capabilities to a text-focused large language model while maintaining or even improving its original text intelligence — a result that contradicts the common assumption that multimodal expansion dilutes core capabilities.

title: Relational Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Pricing in High-Speed Railway Markets
authors: Enrique Adrian Villarrubia-Martin, David Muñoz-Valero, Luis Rodriguez-Benitez, Giovanni Montana, Luis Jimenez-Linares
summary: This paper addresses dynamic pricing in liberalized railway markets using multi-agent reinforcement learning with graph-based relational reasoning. Railway operators must set prices in competitive environments with partial information and no direct communication allowed, making strategic inference from market data alone difficult. The authors propose a novel approach that models the market as a graph of operational entities and use graph neural networks with attention mechanisms to learn competitive interactions, achieving higher revenue and stability than existing baselines.

title: Square-Root Price Impact Is Necessary for Endogenous Manipulation Cycles in Learning-Agent Markets
authors: Yang Zhou, Jianwen Chen, Ruipeng Wei
summary: This paper reveals how a single institutional trading agent, optimized through evolution, spontaneously discovers predatory manipulation strategies in a market with thousands of retail traders. The key finding is mathematical: the manipulation cycles arise from nonlinear dynamics and critically depend on *square-root price impact* — a specific relationship between order size and price movement. Without this property, the cycles vanish entirely, suggesting that real market microstructure features (not just agent greed) enable large-scale market manipulation.

title: TACTIC-KG: Toward Small Agent Teams for Cyber Threat Intelligence Knowledge Graph Construction
authors: Mouhamed Amine Bouchiha, Gregory Blanc
summary: This paper presents TACTIC-KG, a multi-agent framework that converts unstructured cybersecurity threat reports into structured knowledge graphs by breaking down the task into specialized sub-tasks (extraction, typing, verification, curation) handled by smaller language models. Rather than using one large model to do everything, the authors show that smaller 3–8B parameter models working together as agents produce better results — higher accuracy, more stable performance, and lower deployment costs — compared to single large models attempting the same work.

title: LLM for the development of FCM
authors: Alexis Kafantaris
summary: This paper demonstrates how a local large language model (Qwen2.5-32B) can automatically extract quantitative relationships from text to build fuzzy cognitive maps — a type of knowledge graph that models cause-and-effect connections between concepts. The authors validate their approach on hotel reviews, showing that the resulting map can predict customer satisfaction ratings without being explicitly trained on them.

title: Look-Ahead-Freedom as Temporal Non-Interference: A Verifiable Correctness Property for Backtesting and Agentic Trading Pipelines
authors: Xavier Fonseca
summary: This paper formalizes look-ahead bias — a critical problem in backtesting where future information leaks into past decisions — as a temporal non-interference property in formal logic. The authors develop a type system that can automatically verify whether a trading pipeline is free from look-ahead contamination, solving a problem that the industry currently manages with ad-hoc checks and empirical detectors that miss real leaks.

title: Probably Correct Optimal Stable Matching under Two-Sided Uncertainty
authors: Andreas Athanasopoulos, Anne-Marie George, Christos Dimitrakakis
summary: This paper studies how to learn and identify the optimal stable matching in two-sided markets (like job–candidate or student–school assignments) when both sides' preferences are unknown and must be learned from noisy feedback. The key innovation is handling *two-sided uncertainty* — where neither side reveals their full preferences upfront — while still finding the best matching efficiently. The work matters because real matching markets operate with incomplete information, and this provides theoretical guarantees on how many queries are needed to find a provably good solution.

title: Context-Constrained Transfer Learning for Tabular Foundation Models via Data Distillation
authors: Yijun Lin, Sai Li
summary: This paper addresses a key limitation of Tabular Foundation Models (TFMs)—neural networks trained on diverse tabular datasets to serve as general-purpose predictors—when applied to transfer learning tasks. The core problem: TFMs struggle when moving to new datasets because they have strict limits on how much context (training data) they can use, and they're sensitive to distribution shifts. The authors propose TL-ANDI, a method that distills a source dataset down to its most informative samples using optimal transport, then fine-tunes the model with those compressed samples plus local label refinement. This matters because it makes TFMs practical for real-world tabular problems where data is heterogeneous and budgets are tight.

title: An Exploration of Agentic Information Fusion for Test Maintenance Prediction
authors: Jingxiong Liu, Nasser Mohammadiha, Gregory Gay
summary: This paper presents MAST, a multi-agent framework that predicts which test cases need maintenance after production code changes. Test maintenance is expensive and time-consuming as codebases evolve, so automating the identification of affected tests has immediate practical value. The key innovation is combining multiple analysis techniques—static code analysis, lexical similarity, and semantic understanding—through intelligent fusion rather than relying on any single approach.

title: Multi-Turn On-Policy Distillation with Prefix Replay
authors: Baohao Liao, Hanze Dong, Christof Monz, Xinxing Xu, Li Dong et al.
summary: This paper tackles the problem of training smaller language models (students) to imitate larger ones (teachers) as they interact with environments over multiple steps — a task called on-policy distillation. The key insight is that standard approaches require expensive repeated queries to both the teacher and the environment; instead, the authors propose ReOPD, which reuses pre-collected teacher trajectories and has the student practice on selected steps, making training 4× faster while matching or improving performance.

title: Heaviside Continuity of Rolling Coefficients for Eliminating Epistemic Entropy in Large Language Models
authors: MY Pitsane, Hope Mogale
summary: This paper introduces HCRC (Heaviside Continuity of Rolling Coefficients), a framework that prevents large language models from producing incorrect outputs by checking intermediate reasoning steps before allowing the model to proceed. Instead of modifying the model itself, HCRC wraps inference with a "gate" that validates each step using independent verification signals — if verification fails, execution stops rather than propagating errors downstream. The work matters because it shows that reliability in LLM reasoning can come from execution control rather than just building bigger models.

title: Order Splitting and Liquidity Replenishment Are Jointly Necessary for the Square-Root Law of Market Impact:
authors: Yang Zhou, Jianwen Chen, Ruipeng Wei
summary: This paper explains why market impact follows a square-root law — a universal empirical pattern where large trades move prices by roughly the square root of their size. The authors use a simulated limit-order-book market with heterogeneous trading agents to show that two specific mechanisms — order splitting (breaking large trades into smaller pieces) and liquidity replenishment by market makers — are jointly necessary and sufficient to produce this law. Testing three competing theoretical models, they find all three fail in isolation, but their agent-based model matches real market data from the Tokyo Stock Exchange when both mechanisms are present.

title: Deep Learning for Dynamic Programming with Recursive Utility
authors: Xianhua Peng, Wu Guo
summary: This paper introduces the Certainty Equivalent Learning (CEL) algorithm, a deep learning method for solving high-dimensional dynamic programming problems where agents have recursive utility preferences—a realistic model of how humans actually make long-term financial decisions. The key innovation is learning the "certainty equivalent" (a value that makes people indifferent between a certain payoff and a risky one) directly with neural networks, which sidesteps the numerical difficulties that have made these problems intractable before. The method works without knowing the explicit form of the utility function or requiring differentiable state transitions, making it practical for real economic and financial problems.

title: Governing Generative AI Across Financial Institutions: An SR 26-2-Compatible Framework for Generative AI Risk Control
authors: Yiqing Wang, Yixin Kang, Luyun Lin, Siqi Mao
summary: This paper proposes a governance framework (GAICF) for using generative AI in regulated financial institutions, designed to comply with the U.S. Federal Reserve's new SR 26-2 supervisory guidance on model risk management. The work addresses a critical gap: while SR 26-2 modernizes how banks must manage AI models, it explicitly excludes generative AI—yet these tools (like LLMs) are increasingly embedded in banking workflows where their outputs influence credit decisions, compliance documentation, and regulatory explanations. The paper matters because it provides financial institutions with a practical roadmap to govern generative AI use while staying aligned with regulatory expectations.

title: A Spectral Generalisation of the Variance Ratio: Eigenstructure of Long-Horizon Portfolio Covariance and a Multi-Memory Factor Model of U.S. Equity Returns
authors: Anders G Frøseth
